1:
‘And he came into his own country; and his disciples follow him.’ The witnesses agree this is the visit to Nazareth, the place of his upbringing, where he met rejection.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the scene. Robertson holds there is ‘no real reason’ to identify this with the earlier rejection of Luke 4:16-31; it was ‘not incongruous that Jesus should give Nazareth a second chance,’ visiting mother, brothers and sisters again. Barnes refers to the parallel in Matthew 13:54-58.

The fathers probe the motive and the offence. Theophylact suggests the Lord returned ‘not that he was ignorant they would despise him, but that they might have no reason to say, If thou hadst come, we had believed thee.’ Bede marvels at ‘the blindness of the Nazarenes,’ who despised him ‘solely on account of his kindred’ though his words and works might have shown him to be the Christ. Pseudo-Jerome notes that ‘oftentimes the origin of a man brings him contempt’ (compare 1 Samuel 25:10).

The Puritan Henry observes that, though he had been in danger of his life there (Luke 4:29), Christ came among them again, ‘so strangely doth he wait to be gracious,’ and his disciples followed him into danger, ‘for they had left all to follow him.’ The shared, dominant note is that Christ returns to the town of his upbringing, his disciples with him, only to be despised by those who knew his family and trade — the prelude to the unbelief that would limit his mighty works among them. Bede notes that by ‘his own country’ is meant Nazareth, where he was brought up, and Augustine harmonises Matthew’s ‘the carpenter’s son’ with Mark’s ‘the carpenter,’ since both might truly be said of him; the witnesses agree that Christ’s return to the town of his upbringing, his disciples following, was the prelude to the unbelief that would limit his mighty works among his own. Pseudo-Jerome adds that the Lord, called the son of a workman, is despised for his origin, as it is written that God hath respect unto the lowly but beholdeth the proud afar off.

The witnesses thus frame the whole episode as a study in the peril of the familiar. Theophylact notes that even “if the prophet has noble relations, his countrymen hate them, and on that account do not honour the prophet,” so that nearness breeds not reverence but contempt. Robertson adds that, though neither Mark nor Matthew names Nazareth here, “it is plain that by” patris the town of his upbringing is meant. And Henry presses the devotion of the Twelve, who “had left all, to follow him whithersoever he went,” even back into a place where his life had been threatened — so that the disciples’ loyalty stands in quiet rebuke of the townsmen’s scorn, the strangers cleaving to him whom his own would not receive.
2:
‘Many hearing him were astonished, saying, Whence hath this man these things?’ The witnesses agree the Nazarenes’ amazement was mingled with a sting of contempt.

The grammatical-historical commentators catch the tone. Robertson renders the curt question — ‘Whence these things to this fellow?’ — ‘with a sting and a fling in their words,’ the imperfect tense showing they ‘continued to be amazed’ yet challenged both the wisdom and the mighty works wrought by his hands. Ellicott notes the better text reads ‘the many,’ the majority present, and that the ‘mighty works’ must refer to what had been reported, since none were done at Nazareth (v.5). Clarke notes the addition ‘at his doctrine’ in some manuscripts.

The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here in fact concerns the sealing of the sepulchre and so does not bear on the verse. Gill observes it was on the sabbath, when the city had come together, that he taught in the synagogue.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: the townsmen could not deny the wisdom of his teaching or the report of his works, yet their astonishment soured into a contemptuous ‘whence?’ They asked the right question — the source of his wisdom and power — but in the wrong spirit, refusing the obvious answer because they knew his humble family and trade. Their wonder, which should have led to faith, became instead the seedbed of the offence that the next verses record, so that familiarity with his lowly origin blinded them to the glory before their eyes. Robertson notes the imperfect tense, that they ‘continued to be amazed,’ challenging both the wisdom and the powers wrought by his hands; the witnesses agree their wonder, which should have led to faith, curdled into a contemptuous ‘whence?’ because they knew his humble family and trade, and so familiarity blinded them to the glory before their eyes. Ellicott notes the better text reads ‘the many,’ the majority present, and that the mighty works they named must refer to what had been reported, since none were wrought at Nazareth itself.

The verse, the witnesses agree, sets the whole tragedy of unbelief in miniature. The Nazarenes confess three things at once — the “wisdom” given him, the “mighty works” wrought by his hands, and that these are beyond all they can account for — and every one of these confessions ought to have led them to faith. Robertson hears the curt “whence” as almost a sneer, “Whence these things to this fellow?”; the wonder that should have opened into worship closed instead into resentment. So the witnesses read in their astonishment not the dawning of belief but its refusal: men may grant the wisdom and the works of Christ, and yet, stumbling at his lowly origin, turn the very evidence that should convince them into a ground of offence.
3:
‘Is not this the carpenter, the son of Mary?’ The witnesses agree the Nazarenes stumbled at Christ’s lowly trade and family, and note that Mark alone calls him ‘the carpenter.’

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the trade. Robertson and Ellicott note that where Matthew has ‘the carpenter’s son’ (Matthew 13:55), Mark alone calls Jesus himself ‘the carpenter’ — ‘he was both,’ carrying on the business after Joseph’s death; the word tekton means a worker in wood, and Justin Martyr speaks of ploughs and yokes made by Jesus. Lightfoot and Clarke cite the Jewish maxim that a father must teach his son a trade, ‘he who teaches not his son to do some work is as if he taught him robbery.’

Gill multiplies rabbinic examples of great doctors who plied a trade — Jochanan a shoemaker, Isaac a blacksmith — so that the Jews had no ground to despise Christ for his work; he suggests Jesus followed it ‘to bear that part of the first Adam’s curse’ of eating bread by the sweat of his brow. Wesley simply affirms ‘there can be no doubt’ he wrought with Joseph in his youth.

The shared, dominant note is that the very humility of Christ’s earthly life became the occasion of his rejection: knowing him as the village carpenter, the son of Mary, the brother of James and Joses and Jude and Simon, the Nazarenes were ‘offended’ (Matthew 11:6). The witnesses see in this both a witness to the reality of the Incarnation — the Lord of glory truly took a workman’s life — and a warning against the unbelief that despises God’s gifts because they come in lowly guise. Robertson notes Justin Martyr speaks of ploughs and yokes made by Jesus, and that he carried on the business after Joseph’s death; the witnesses agree the very humility of Christ’s earthly life became the occasion of his rejection, a witness at once to the reality of the Incarnation and a warning against the unbelief that despises God’s gifts because they come in lowly guise. Lightfoot and Clarke cite the Jewish maxim that a father must teach his son a trade, ‘he who teaches not his son to work is as if he taught him robbery’; so Joseph brought up Jesus to his own craft.

Robertson presses the trade itself: the word tekton, “a very old word, from Homer down,” was “originally applied to the worker in wood or builder with wood,” then to any craftsman; and Jesus, since Joseph’s death, “had carried on the business and was ‘the carpenter’ of Nazareth.” Mark’s listing of the brothers — James, Joses, Judas, Simon — and the sisters known in the town drives the offence home: every name in the catalogue was a reason, to their minds, why this man could not be more than they knew. So the witnesses find the deepest irony of Nazareth here: the very household ties that should have endeared him made him incredible, and the lowliness he had embraced for love’s sake became, to his neighbours, the unanswerable objection to his glory.
4:
‘A prophet is not without honour, but in his own country, and among his own kin, and in his own house.’ Only three voices comment, briefly, so the synthesis is short; they agree the proverb states the common fate of the prophet despised by his own.

The grammatical-historical Barnes refers to the parallels in Matthew 13:57 and John 4:44, where the same saying explains the Nazarenes’ rejection. The Wesleyan Clarke likewise directs the reader to the fuller treatment at Matthew 13:55-58. Gill notes Christ spoke ‘the following proverb’ in answer to their offence.

With so few and so brief comments the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is clear and the witnesses are agreed. Christ meets the contempt of his townsmen not with anger but with a proverb that explains it: the prophet is honoured everywhere but at home, where familiarity with his origin and kindred breeds a contempt that blinds men to his true dignity. Mark sharpens the saying beyond the other Evangelists by adding ‘among his own kin, and in his own house,’ so that the circle of rejection narrows from the country to the family. The proverb thus both accounts for the Nazarenes’ unbelief and consoles every servant of God who finds, as the Master did, that those nearest by blood are often furthest in faith; the honour withheld at home will be given by God and by the wider world, and the rejection itself is no disproof but rather the predicted lot of the prophet. Gill notes Christ spoke the proverb in answer to their offence, and the witnesses agree it both accounts for the Nazarenes’ unbelief and consoles every servant of God who finds, as the Master did, that those nearest by blood are often furthest in faith, the honour withheld at home being given by God and by the wider world. Clarke directs the reader to the fuller treatment of the saying at Matthew 13, where the same proverb explains why the Nazarenes, knowing his kindred, took offence at him. Gill adds that Christ spoke the proverb directly in answer to their offence, and the witnesses agree the rejection itself was no disproof of his mission but the very lot the proverb predicts for the prophet at home.

Mark’s form of the proverb, the witnesses note, is the fullest, adding to “his own country” both “his own kin” and “his own house” — three narrowing circles in which honour fades as nearness grows. The saying is not a complaint but a calm reading of the human heart, which slights what it has long had close at hand and prizes what comes from afar. It places the rejection at Nazareth within a long pattern: the prophets before him had met the same dishonour from their own, and Christ, in owning the proverb, claims his place among them. The townsmen’s offence, far from disproving his mission, only confirmed it — he was treated exactly as God’s messengers had always been treated by those who knew them best and believed them least.
5:
‘And he could there do no mighty work, save that he laid his hands upon a few sick folk, and healed them.’ The few witnesses agree the ‘could not’ means ‘would not,’ for it was their unbelief, not his weakness, that withheld the miracles.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the fuller description — the laying on of hands upon a few sick folk — is peculiar to Mark, where Matthew says simply ‘not many miracles.’ The Reformed Gill explains the Hebrew idiom: it was ‘not fit and proper’ that he should work miracles there, given their prejudice, ‘not but that he could if he would’; he compares the like idiom used even of God (Genesis 19:22, Mark 9:23).

The Wesleyan Wesley puts it sharply: ‘he could do no miracle there… consistently with his wisdom and goodness,’ it being inconsistent with his wisdom to work them where they could not promote his end, and with his goodness to give more evidence to men who would only have it increase their condemnation.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the ‘could not’ is no limit on Christ’s power but a statement of its moral conditions. He withheld his mighty works at Nazareth not from inability but because their settled unbelief made such works unfitting — to multiply evidence among those resolved to reject it would only deepen their guilt. Yet even there his compassion broke through, healing a few sick folk on whom he laid his hands. The witnesses see in this both the sovereignty of grace, which is not constrained to display itself where it is despised, and the solemn truth that unbelief impoverishes those who harbour it, shutting the door against the very blessings Christ stood ready to give. Ellicott notes the laying on of hands upon a few sick folk is peculiar to Mark, where Matthew says simply ‘not many miracles’; the witnesses agree the ‘could not’ is no limit on Christ’s power but a statement of its moral conditions, his compassion still breaking through to heal a few even where unbelief shut the door against greater blessings.

So the witnesses guard the verse from misreading. The “could not” is no confession of impotence but, as Gill says, a Hebrew way of saying a thing is “not fit” to be done; as Wesley urges, it was “not consistent with his wisdom and goodness” to lavish miracles where they would only harden, and so heap up condemnation. The few that he did — laying his hands on a few sick folk and healing them — stand as tokens that mercy was not wholly withdrawn even from the unbelieving town. The limit, then, lay not in the Healer but in the hearts that would not have him: where faith opened a door, power flowed freely; where unbelief shut it, the same power, in wisdom and in judgement, held back.
6:
‘And he marvelled because of their unbelief. And he went round about the villages, teaching.’ The witnesses agree Christ’s wonder at unbelief bears on the reality of his human nature, and that he turned from Nazareth to teach the villages.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the marvelling. Ellicott notes the word ‘is to be noted as bearing on the reality of our Lord’s human nature,’ the wonder men feel at the unlooked-for; Robertson observes Jesus marvelled at the centurion’s faith where none was expected (Luke 7:9) and here at the lack of faith where he had a right to expect it, even ‘in his own home.’ Gill notes it was a surprise to Christ as man that his countrymen should reject him.

The fathers draw out the turn to the villages. Theophylact teaches us ‘not to despise little things… but to sow the word of the Lord in abandoned and lowly villages,’ and reads the sending two-by-two from Ecclesiastes 4:9 (‘two are better than one’). Bede notes the Lord ‘did not grudge his servants his own virtues,’ giving them power as he himself had healed.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ, having marvelled at the unbelief of his own town, did not cease his work but went round the villages teaching — and prepared to extend his ministry through the Twelve. The witnesses see in his marvelling a genuine mark of his true manhood, and in his turning to the lowly villages a lesson that the gospel is not to be confined to great places but sown everywhere, the rejection at Nazareth becoming the occasion for a wider sowing of the word. Gill notes it was a surprise to Christ as man that his countrymen should reject him, and Robertson that he ‘went round about the villages teaching’ in his frequent itineration; the witnesses see in his marvelling a genuine mark of his true manhood, and in his turning to the lowly villages the lesson that the gospel is to be sown everywhere, the rejection at Nazareth becoming the occasion for a wider sowing of the word.

Robertson sets the wonder in its sharpest light: Jesus “marvelled at the faith of the Roman centurion where one would not expect faith” (Matthew 8:10; Luke 7:9), and “here he marvels at the lack of faith where he had a right to expect it.” Yet the Lord does not abandon the work for the affront: he “went round about the villages, teaching.” Theophylact draws the abiding lesson — “not to despise little things, nor always to seek for great cities, but to sow the word of the Lord in abandoned and lowly villages.” So the witnesses join the wonder and the round of labour: rejected at home, Christ turns not to sulk but to sow, carrying to the obscure hamlets the word that his own town had spurned, and teaching his servants to do the same.
7:
‘And he called unto him the twelve, and began to send them forth by two and two; and gave them power over unclean spirits.’ The witnesses agree Christ now commissions the Twelve, pairing them for mutual support and arming them with his own authority.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the sending. Barnes and Clarke note the pairing ‘by two and two’ so that they might ‘encourage and support each other’ and show that ‘union among the ministers of the gospel is essential’; Barnes adds that Christ ‘consulted at the same time the happiness and usefulness of his disciples.’ Robertson notes the Hebraistic doubling, that the six pairs could ‘cover Galilee in six different directions,’ and the imperfect ‘kept on giving’ them authority. Ellicott observes Mark omits the bulk of the discourse of Matthew 10, being the Evangelist of action rather than speech.

The fathers read the pairing morally. Gregory holds they were sent two-by-two because ‘there are two precepts of charity… and charity cannot be between less than two,’ so that ‘he who has not charity towards his neighbour ought in no way to take upon himself the office of preaching.’

The Puritan Henry notes the disciples ‘had received, that they might give; had learned, that they might teach,’ and were now set to work according to their present ability. The shared, dominant note is that Christ, having taught and trained the Twelve, now sends them out as an extension of his own ministry — paired for fellowship and strength, endowed with his authority over the unclean spirits — so that the work of the kingdom begins to spread through his appointed messengers, who go not in their own power but in his. Robertson notes Mark ‘began to send them forth’ (aorist) and ‘kept on giving’ (imperfect) them authority over the unclean spirits throughout the tour; the witnesses agree that Christ, having taught and trained the Twelve, now sends them out as an extension of his own ministry, paired for fellowship and strength, going not in their own power but in his. Gregory reads the pairing morally, that they were sent two by two because charity needs two, and none without love of neighbour should take up the office of preaching.

Clarke presses that “union among the ministers of the Gospel is essential to the promotion of the cause of truth” (cf. Luke 10:1, where the Seventy are likewise sent in pairs). Bede marks the dignity of the gift: as Christ “had healed every sickness… so also He gave the same power to His disciples” — yet “great is the difference between giving and receiving,” for whatever the Lord does “is done in His own power,” while the apostles act only by power received. So the witnesses gather the lesson of the sending: the Twelve go out neither alone nor in their own strength, but two by two for mutual support, and armed with a borrowed authority over the unclean spirits — the first rehearsal of the worldwide mission that would one day be theirs in the power of the risen Lord.
8:
‘That they should take nothing for their journey, save a staff only.’ The witnesses agree the charge enjoined a travelling-light simplicity, and grapple with the famous discrepancy between Mark’s ‘a staff’ and the other Gospels’ ‘no staff.’

The grammatical-historical commentators face the crux. Robertson notes that Mark allows a staff while Matthew 10:10 and Luke 9:3 forbid one; Grotius suggests ‘no second staff,’ and Swete that Matthew and Luke report ‘an early exaggeration of the sternness of the command,’ while Bruce thinks Mark preserves the meaning most clearly. Ellicott and Clarke explain ‘no money’ as no brass or bronze coin (the ‘farthing’ and ‘mite’ of Mark 12:42). Barnes notes Mark omits the ban on going to Gentiles, writing for Gentile readers.

Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background of the scrip (the wallet for victuals on a journey), and the Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced, treating a salutation rather than the verse.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ sent the Twelve out unencumbered, to depend on God and on the hospitality of those they served. The witnesses agree the apparent contradiction over the staff is resolved either by distinguishing a single staff from a second, or by reading the variants as different emphases of the one command to austere simplicity. The point that stands, the grammatical commentators insist, is the spirit rather than the letter: the messengers were to go without provision or display, trusting that their wants would be supplied, so that nothing might encumber their haste or distract them from their errand. Ellicott and Clarke explain ‘no money’ as no brass or bronze coin, the farthing and mite of Mark 12:42, and Barnes that Mark omits the ban on going to Gentiles, writing for Gentile readers; the witnesses agree the point that stands is the spirit rather than the letter — the messengers were to go unencumbered, trusting that their wants would be supplied, that nothing might distract them from their errand. Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background of the scrip, the wallet in which a traveller laid up victuals for his journey, which the messengers were here forbidden to carry.

Lightfoot adds rabbinic colour to the “scrip,” the traveller’s or beggar’s bag that a wandering teacher might carry; Barnes notes the “money” forbidden was the small “piece of brass money” of the purse. The point on which all converge, beneath their differing solutions to the staff, is the studied lightness of the charge: the messengers were to make no provision, to carry nothing that might tie them to the world or tempt them to delay, but to go just as they were. Their want of scrip and bread and money was itself a sermon — a visible witness that they trusted the God who sent them and the hospitality of those who would receive the word, and that the kingdom they preached set them free from the anxious hoarding of earthly goods.
9:
‘But be shod with sandals; and not put on two coats.’ The few witnesses agree the charge continues the call to travelling simplicity, and note the distinction between sandals and shoes.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the footwear. Clarke and Ellicott describe the sandal (Acts 12:8) as the lighter wear of the poor — sole of leather or wood fastened by thongs — distinguished from the ‘shoes’ (short boots) forbidden in Matthew 10:10; Clarke derives the word from a root meaning ‘a thin shoe.’ Robertson notes the ‘two coats’ (two tunics) were ‘a sign of comparative wealth.’

The Wesleyan Wesley explains the contrast: the disciples might wear the sandals they usually had, but not the heavier boots used on long journeys; and he draws the larger lesson that Christ ‘intended by this mission to initiate them into their apostolic work,’ teaching them to trust the care of God who would let them lack nothing (Luke 22:35). Gill discusses whether sandals and shoes differ at all, or whether the prohibition was of two pairs.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: the messengers were to go in the plainest footwear and a single tunic, without the second garment that marked a man of means. The point, as with the staff, is the spirit of unencumbered dependence: they were to travel light, taking no thought for provision, that they might learn, as Wesley observes, the faithful care of God who supplied their every want when they went out trusting him — a lesson the apostles would recall with gratitude all their lives. Gill discusses whether sandals and shoes differ at all, or whether the prohibition was of two pairs, and Robertson that the ‘two coats’ were a sign of comparative wealth; the witnesses agree the messengers were to travel in the plainest footwear and a single tunic, the spirit of the charge being unencumbered dependence on the God who would let them lack nothing. Wesley draws the larger lesson that the mission was meant to initiate the Twelve into their apostolic work, teaching them by experience the faithful care of God who let them lack nothing.

So the witnesses read the footwear and the single coat as one lesson in apostolic simplicity. Clarke explains that the sandal “covered only the sole of the foot,” the plainest of coverings, fit for the road; Wesley adds that the command was a kind of initiation — “an encouragement to them all their days” that the God who sent them out so bare would surely provide. Whatever the precise reconciling of Mark’s sandal with Matthew’s “no shoes,” the charge is of a piece: enough for the journey, nothing for display or for a second store. The messengers of the kingdom were to travel as men whose treasure lay elsewhere, their very dress preaching the trust and the freedom from grasping that their message required of all who would receive it.
10:
‘In what place soever ye enter into an house, there abide till ye depart from that place.’ The three voices comment briefly, so the synthesis is short; they agree the charge enjoins a settled, contented lodging.

The grammatical-historical Robertson explains the meaning: the messengers ‘were to avoid a restless and dissatisfied manner and to take pains in choosing a home’ (so Matthew 10:11, Luke 9:4); it is ‘not a prohibition against accepting invitations,’ but against moving from house to house seeking better entertainment. Gill notes Christ ‘continued giving orders and directions to them.’

With only three brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. The disciple, once received into a house, was to remain there throughout his stay in that town, neither restlessly seeking more comfortable quarters nor giving offence by appearing to despise his first host. The charge guards both the dignity of the messenger and the feelings of those who received him: to flit from house to house in search of better fare would betray a greed and instability unbecoming the gospel, and would slight the hospitality first offered.

The shared, dominant note is that contentment and constancy were to mark the conduct of the Twelve in the homes that received them. Having gone out without provision, depending on the hospitality of others, they were not to abuse that dependence by restless self-indulgence but to abide where they were welcomed, that their single concern might be the work of preaching and healing, not the comfort of their lodging — a simplicity and stability of life that matched the simplicity of their equipment. Robertson stresses it is ‘not a prohibition against accepting invitations’ but against a restless, dissatisfied flitting from house to house; the witnesses agree that contentment and constancy were to mark the conduct of the Twelve in the homes that received them, that their single concern might be the work of preaching and healing, not the comfort of their lodging. Gill notes that Christ continued giving them directions, and the witnesses agree the charge guarded both the dignity of the messenger and the feelings of the host who first received him.

So the witnesses draw from the brief command a lesson in gospel manners. The preacher is to be neither a burden nor a wanderer, moving house to house in search of better entertainment; where he is welcomed, there he abides, content with what is offered, honouring the household that opened its door, until his work in that place is done. Robertson rightly guards against pressing it too far — “it is not a prohibition against accepting invitations” — but the spirit is plain: the messenger seeks not his own comfort nor gives the appearance of slighting his first host. The single, steadfast lodging becomes a small parable of the singleness of heart with which the whole mission was to be carried, free of restlessness and of the grasping after ease.
11:
‘Whosoever shall not receive you… shake off the dust under your feet for a testimony against them.’ The witnesses agree the symbolic act warns those who reject the gospel of a solemn judgment.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the gesture. Robertson notes ‘for a testimony unto them’ (the dative of disadvantage) carries the same force as Luke’s ‘against them’ (Luke 9:5), the ‘dramatic figure of shaking out’ the dust (compare Acts 13:51). Clarke notes the better text reads ‘whatsoever place shall not receive you,’ and that the closing clause about Sodom is probably transferred here from Matthew 10:15. Ellicott refers to the fuller note there.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a catechetical passage on the ways of gaining eternal life, and so does not bear on the verse. Gill explains the act was toward those who would ‘neither take them into their houses, nor hear what they had to say.’

The shared, dominant note is that the messengers, rejected, were to perform a prophetic sign — shaking the very dust of the place from their feet — as a testimony that they had discharged their errand and that the guilt now lay wholly on those who refused them. The witnesses see in this both a warning of judgment, more tolerable for Sodom than for the town that spurns the gospel, and a comfort to the faithful messenger: he is not responsible for the reception of his message, only for its faithful delivery, and may leave the despisers, with a clear conscience, to the God whose word they have rejected. Clarke notes the closing clause about Sodom is probably transferred here from Matthew 10:15, and Gill that the act was toward those who would neither receive nor hear them; the witnesses agree the prophetic sign both warned the despisers of a judgment more tolerable for Sodom and assured the faithful messenger that, having delivered his message, he was not responsible for its reception. Robertson notes the dramatic figure of shaking off the dust (compare Acts 13:51), the dative carrying the same force as Luke’s ‘against them,’ a testimony that the guilt now lay wholly on the rejecters.

So the witnesses read the sign with sobriety. To shake the very dust of a town from one’s feet (so also Acts 13:51) was to treat it as a heathen place, its soil unclean, and to disclaim all further responsibility for the message it had spurned. It was not spite but testimony — a visible witness that the messengers had done their part, and that the guilt of refusal now lay wholly on the heads of those who would not hear. The kingdom of God draws near even to those who reject it, and the nearer it comes the heavier is the account of refusing it. The dust shaken off in silence becomes, at the last, a dreadful witness against the place that would not receive the word of God.
12:
‘And they went out, and preached that men should repent.’ The witnesses agree the Twelve carried out their commission, their message continuing that of John and of Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the mission. Robertson notes the constative aorist summarising the tour, and that ‘repent’ was the message of the Baptist (Matthew 3:2) and of Jesus (Mark 1:15). Barnes explains they called men to repent ‘because sin was evil… and at the presence of the Messiah it was fit that the nation should turn from its sins.’ Ellicott observes their work was ‘a continuation of that of the Baptist’: they announced the kingdom and repentance, but ‘did not as yet proclaim their Master as being himself the Christ.’

The Reformed Calvin notes Mark and Luke relate what Matthew passes over — that the apostles ‘proceeded to execute the commission,’ going through the cities and villages and soon returning, which shows the office then bestowed was ‘temporary, and indeed lasted but a few days.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the Twelve, sent out in pairs and unencumbered, went forth and preached repentance. Their message, the grammatical commentators stress, was at this stage the same as the Baptist’s and the Master’s call to turn from sin in view of the nearing kingdom, not yet the full proclamation of Jesus as the Christ — that would await his death and resurrection. The witnesses see in this first apostolic tour both the beginning of the gospel’s spread through Christ’s messengers and a temporary commission, a training and a foretaste of the wider work to come. Barnes explains they called men to repent because sin was evil and the Messiah’s presence made it fit the nation should turn from its sins; the witnesses agree this first apostolic tour was both the beginning of the gospel’s spread through Christ’s messengers and a temporary commission, a training and a foretaste of the wider work to come after his resurrection. Calvin notes that Mark and Luke relate what Matthew omits, that the apostles executed their commission, going through the villages and soon returning, the office being temporary.

So the witnesses set the apostles’ first preaching in its true light: a brief, preparatory mission, echoing the one urgent cry of John and of Jesus — “repent” (Mark 1:15). Barnes notes the ground of the call: men were to turn from their sins “because sin was evil… and because at his presence it was fit that the nation should turn.” Calvin marks that the office was temporary, lasting but a few days till they returned. The fuller proclamation of the cross and the risen Lord would wait; for now the message was the old summons to amendment of life, pressed on a people whose King had come among them. The Twelve went, two by two, as heralds of repentance, preparing Israel for the Christ already in their midst.
13:
‘And they cast out many devils, and anointed with oil many that were sick, and healed them.’ The witnesses agree the apostles confirmed their preaching by miracles, and note that Mark alone mentions the anointing with oil.

The grammatical-historical commentators discuss the oil. Robertson, Barnes and Ellicott note this is the only New Testament parallel to James 5:14, and weigh whether the oil was used as medicine (the best remedy of the ancients, often applied after bathing) or as a symbol of divine healing, like Christ’s use of clay (John 9:6); Ellicott holds it united ‘the use of natural outward means… with prayer for the divine blessing.’ All insist it had ‘not the slightest affinity’ with the medieval sacrament of extreme unction.

Lightfoot, with Beza, argues the oil was ‘a symbol of that miraculous power, not a medicament,’ while granting that anointing the sick was a common Jewish practice. Clarke agrees it ‘could avail no more of itself than the imposition of hands,’ being used ‘symbolically, as an emblem of that ease, comfort, and joy’ they prayed God to impart.

The shared, dominant note is that the Twelve, going out in Christ’s authority, cast out demons and healed the sick, anointing them with oil. The witnesses debate whether the oil was medicine, symbol, or both, but agree on two things: that the cure came not from the oil’s natural virtue but from the supernatural blessing of God, as Wesley insists; and that this apostolic anointing, echoed in James, is wholly distinct from the later rite of extreme unction administered to the dying. The oil was an outward sign attending faith and prayer, the visible accompaniment of a healing power that was Christ’s alone. Lightfoot, with Beza, argues the oil was ‘a symbol of that miraculous power, not a medicament,’ while granting anointing the sick was a common Jewish practice; the witnesses agree the cure came not from the oil’s natural virtue but from the supernatural blessing of God, and that this apostolic anointing, echoed in James, is wholly distinct from the later rite of extreme unction administered to the dying.

Clarke adds that the oil was used “symbolically, as an emblem of that ease, comfort, and joy” the apostles prayed God to give the sick, availing “no more of itself than the imposition of hands.” The witnesses thus hold the act together while dividing gently on its nature — medicine to some, sacramental sign to others, and to all a vehicle of God’s healing power — but they are of one voice against the medieval rite of extreme unction, which Wesley sharply distinguishes: the apostolic anointing healed the living body by “the supernatural blessing of God” (James 5:14), whereas the later sacrament is administered to the dying. The casting out of devils and the anointing alike were the visible seals of the authority Christ had lent the Twelve, confirming their word with works of mercy.
14:
‘And king Herod heard… It is John the Baptist; he is risen from the dead.’ The witnesses agree the apostles’ tour spread Christ’s fame even to the palace, where a guilty Herod concluded that the John he beheaded had risen.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Robertson notes the tour ‘wakened all Galilee,’ so that even Herod heard — ‘a palace is late in hearing spiritual news’; the miracles ‘work in him’ (compare John 10:41, John wrought none) being ‘a snatch of Herod’s theology.’ Ellicott cites the Roman poet Persius on the superstitious dread that haunted ‘the feast of Herod’s birthday.’ Barnes refers to the full account in Matthew 14:1-12.

The fathers probe the report. Bede marks ‘how great was the envy of the Jews,’ who would believe John risen, though he did no miracle, rather than confess Jesus risen whom miracles approved. Theophylact and Pseudo-Chrysostom note the people’s guarded surmise — veiling under the name of Moses’s promised prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15) their fear to say openly ‘This is the Christ.’ Augustine harmonises the accounts of Herod’s hesitation.

The Puritan Henry surveys the ‘wild notions’ of the people, who would believe anything rather than that he was the Son of God. The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s spreading fame forced the question of his identity even upon Herod, whose guilty conscience — not sober reasoning — raised the ghost of the murdered Baptist; the people’s varied guesses (John, Elijah, a prophet) and Herod’s dread alike falling short of the truth they feared to own. Robertson notes the tour ‘wakened all Galilee’ so that even the palace heard, and Bede that the Jews would sooner believe John risen, who did no miracle, than confess Jesus risen whom miracles approved; the witnesses agree Christ’s spreading fame forced the question of his identity even upon Herod, whose guilty conscience — not sober reasoning — raised the ghost of the murdered Baptist. Ellicott cites the Roman poet Persius on the superstitious dread that haunted the feast of Herod’s birthday, an apt illustration of the guilty fear that here gripped the king.

Pseudo-Jerome notes that the fame spread because “it is not right that a candle should be placed under a bushel,” while Theophylact recalls how boldly John had “confuted many men” with his “generation of vipers.” The witnesses thus gather a court haunted by its own crime: the tour of the Twelve has carried the name of Jesus even into Herod’s palace, and the tetrarch, hearing of the powers at work, can think only of the prophet he silenced. Bede’s observation cuts deepest — that men would sooner credit the rising of John, who wrought no miracle, than confess the resurrection of Jesus, whom signs and wonders proclaimed. Guilt and unbelief together breed a strange credulity, ready to believe a ghost but not the Son of God.
15:
‘Others said, That it is Elias. And others, That it is a prophet, or as one of the prophets.’ The few voices note the rival popular opinions about Jesus.

The grammatical-historical commentators record the views. Gill explains some thought him Elijah the Tishbite, expected to come before the Messiah; others a prophet like those of old (compare Matthew 16:14). Clarke notes a textual point, that the omission of ‘or’ in the best manuscripts ‘mends the sense.’ Wesley glosses ‘a prophet, as one of the prophets’ as ‘not inferior to one of the ancient prophets.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: the people, unable to deny the mighty works wrought through Jesus, cast about for an explanation that fell short of the truth. Some said Elijah, looking for the forerunner of the Messiah; others, a prophet risen or newly raised, equal to the ancients. Each opinion granted that he was extraordinary, sent of God, yet none confessed him to be the Christ, the Son of God.

The shared, dominant note is that the variety of guesses about Jesus’s identity — John risen, Elijah returned, a prophet of old — displays both the impression his works made and the blindness that would not see the obvious. The witnesses observe that men will entertain almost any theory rather than the simple truth of his deity; the same speculations would reappear when Jesus himself asked the disciples ‘whom do men say that I am?’ (Matthew 16:14), and only the Father’s revelation could bring a man past them to the true confession. Gill explains some looked for Elijah the Tishbite to come before the Messiah, others for a prophet risen or newly raised; the witnesses agree that men will entertain almost any theory rather than the simple truth of his deity, the same speculations reappearing when Jesus asked ‘whom do men say that I am?’ (Matthew 16:14), past which only the Father’s revelation could bring a man. Clarke notes a textual point, that the omission of ‘or’ in the best manuscripts mends the sense, and Wesley that ‘a prophet, as one of the prophets’ means not inferior to the ancients.

The Puritan Henry gathers these “wild notions” into one verdict: men who would not own him as the Son of God were yet “willing to believe any thing rather than the truth.” So the witnesses display the confusion of a people who felt the force of Jesus but would not see who he was. Each guess reached after something extraordinary — the returning Elijah, herald of Messiah; a prophet risen in the old line; or, to Herod’s conscience, the murdered Baptist come again — and each fell short of the one confession that mattered. The verse stands as a quiet measure of how near men may come to the truth about Christ and still miss it, crediting him with every honour except the one that is his due.
16:
‘But when Herod heard thereof, he said, It is John, whom I beheaded: he is risen from the dead.’ The few voices agree Herod’s guilty conscience fixed on this dread conclusion.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that Herod’s fears ‘got the best of him,’ so that he ‘settled down on this nightmare’: he could still see ‘that charger containing John’s head coming towards him in his dreams,’ and the verb (apekephalisa, ‘whom I beheaded’) shows he ‘recognises his guilt.’ The Wesleyan Wesley notes that, hearing the various judgments, Herod ‘still said, It is John.’ Gill notes he spoke on hearing of the different opinions about Christ.

With only three brief voices the synthesis is necessarily short, but the substance is plain and agreed. Amid the people’s several guesses, Herod fastened on the one that his own conscience supplied: the man he had murdered was risen to confront him. Where others speculated about Elijah or a prophet, Herod’s guilt spoke, and he could not shake the conviction that John, whom he had beheaded, now stood before him in the works of Jesus.

The shared, dominant note is that a guilty conscience is its own tormentor. Herod, having silenced the prophet who reproved him, found him risen, as it were, in his fears; the very emphasis of his words — ‘John, whom I beheaded’ — betrays the wound that would not heal. The witnesses see in Herod a standing example of how sin, though it may put away the reprover, cannot put away the reproach, and how the memory of blood unjustly shed haunts the murderer long after the deed is done. Robertson notes the verb ‘whom I beheaded’ shows Herod recognised his guilt, the charger in his dreams; the witnesses agree that a guilty conscience is its own tormentor, Herod finding the prophet he had silenced risen, as it were, in his fears, and the memory of blood unjustly shed haunting the murderer long after the deed. Wesley notes that, hearing the various judgments concerning Jesus, Herod still said ‘It is John,’ his fear fastening on the one explanation his conscience supplied.

Robertson catches the haunted fixity exactly: amid all the opinions, Herod “settled down on this nightmare,” and “could still see that charger containing John’s head coming towards him in his dreams.” The emphatic “whom I beheaded” is the heart of it — the confession of a murderer who cannot escape his deed. Where the people guessed at Elijah or a prophet, Herod’s own crime supplied the answer his conscience could not silence. So the witnesses read in the tetrarch a sober warning: guilt unrepented becomes its own tormentor, peopling the imagination with the ghost of the wronged. The very miracles of Christ, which should have called him to repentance, served only to revive the memory of innocent blood and to fasten upon him a dread he could neither prove nor flee.
17:
‘For Herod himself had sent forth and laid hold upon John, and bound him in prison for Herodias’ sake.’ The witnesses agree Mark now turns back to recount John’s imprisonment for reproving the unlawful marriage.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the history. Robertson notes Mark gives the narrative of John’s death ‘some while before these nervous fears of Herod,’ the news of the murder at Machaerus having seemingly ended the Galilean tour (Matthew 14:12). Ellicott refers to the fuller notes on Matthew 14:3-12.

The fathers draw out the cause and the marvel. Bede recounts the genealogy — Herodias, taken from Philip, married to Herod against the Law — and that John rebuked the unlawful union. Theophylact marks ‘how great is the fury of lust,’ that Herod forgot his awe of John to serve it. Gregory wonders that one ‘filled even in his mother’s womb with the spirit of prophecy… is beheaded for the dancing of a girl,’ concluding God so ‘sinks them’ in this life whom he has chosen for glory. Pseudo-Jerome reads the head of the Law cut off and given to a Gentile damsel.

The Reformed Gill and the Gloss note the better motive in Herod’s earlier restraint — that he ‘feared John, knowing that he was a just man and a holy.’ The shared, dominant note is that John was imprisoned for his faithful reproof of Herod’s adulterous and incestuous marriage; the witnesses see in his suffering the lot of the faithful witness who will not flatter the sins of the great, and in Herod the conflict of a conscience that revered the prophet yet served its lust. Bede recounts the genealogy — Herodias taken from Philip and married to Herod against the Law — and Theophylact marks the fury of lust that made Herod forget his awe of John; the witnesses agree John was imprisoned for his faithful reproof of an adulterous and incestuous marriage, the lot of the witness who will not flatter the sins of the great. Gregory wonders that the greatest born of women, filled with the Spirit from the womb, should be beheaded for a girl’s dancing, concluding God so sinks in this life those whom he has chosen for glory.

Remigius reads the descent of sin: “his lustful will drove him to lay hands on a man whom he knew to be just and holy,” and so “a less fault became the cause to him of a greater” (Revelation 22:11). Gregory marvels that one filled with the Spirit from the womb “is beheaded for the dancing of a girl” — not for any hidden fault, but as the trial of the righteous. So the witnesses open the martyrdom with its cause: a marriage the Law condemned, a prophet who would not be silent, and a king who, to please the woman he had unlawfully taken, bound the man of God in the fortress of Machaerus. The chains were forged of a guilty passion that could not bear reproof — the first act of a tragedy that fidelity to truth would carry to its bloody end.
18:
‘For John had said unto Herod, It is not lawful for thee to have thy brother’s wife.’ The few voices agree John’s plain reproof, grounded in the Law, was the cause of his imprisonment and death.

The grammatical-historical commentators anchor the charge. Barnes points to the Levitical prohibition (Leviticus 18:16); Robertson notes the marriage was unlawful ‘while the brother was alive’ (Leviticus 20:21), though after a brother’s death it was often a duty to marry his widow. Gill stresses that John ‘had been personally with him, and had plainly and freely told him of his sin,’ reproving him for all the evils he had done.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a catechetical passage on the apostles’ dignity above Moses and the prophets, and so does not bear on the verse.

With so brief a body of genuine comment the synthesis is short, but the substance is clear and agreed. John’s offence was simply that he told the truth: he had said to Herod’s face that his union with Herodias, his living brother’s wife, was forbidden by the Law of God. The reproof was not whispered behind the king’s back but spoken plainly to him, the fearless word of a prophet who would not, for safety or favour, connive at a great man’s sin.

The shared, dominant note is that faithful reproof of the powerful, grounded in God’s law, is the duty of his servant whatever the cost. The witnesses see in John the pattern of the courageous witness: he laid the plain commandment of God against the king’s adultery and incest, and for that plainness lost first his liberty and then his life — a rebuke that, as the older writers note, owes no deference to a crown. Robertson notes the marriage was unlawful while the brother lived (Leviticus 20:21), though after a brother’s death it was often a duty to marry his widow; the witnesses agree John’s offence was simply that he told the truth, laying the plain commandment of God against the king’s sin, a rebuke that owes no deference to a crown.

The verse stands as the model of the prophet’s courage. John did not soften the truth for a king, nor weigh the cost to himself; he named the sin by its right name and laid against it the plain commandment of God — it was unlawful, while the brother lived, to take his wife (Leviticus 18:16; Leviticus 20:21). “It is not lawful for thee” — the word that would cost him liberty and life — was spoken to the very man with power to kill him. So the witnesses read in the Baptist the pattern of faithful reproof: not railing, not flattery, but the clear, grave statement of God’s law, addressed to the conscience of the great no less than the small, and held to though chains and the headsman should follow.
19:
‘Therefore Herodias had a quarrel against him, and would have killed him; but she could not.’ The few voices agree Herodias nursed an implacable grudge against the prophet who had reproved her.

The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the language. Ellicott renders it ‘had a grudge, or spite, against him’; Robertson, with a striking idiom, says she ‘had it in for him’ — the imperfect tense describing her settled hostility, that she ‘kept her eye on him’ and ‘never let up, but bided her time’ (compare Genesis 49:23). She ‘desired to kill him,’ yet ‘could not’ — not yet, the power being for a time withheld. Clarke notes the verb means she ‘sought to kill him.’

Gill explains she ‘bore him an inward grudge, and lay in wait to take the first opportunity of dispatching him.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: Herodias, stung by John’s reproof, conceived a murderous hatred and waited only for the chance to act on it. Where Herod feared and revered the prophet, his wife harboured an unrelenting malice, like a second Jezebel toward a second Elijah. The grammatical commentators capture in the imperfect tense the patient persistence of her spite: she did not forget or forgive, but watched and waited.

The shared, dominant note is that the ‘could not’ marks only a delay, not a deliverance. Herodias’s will to murder was fixed; what restrained her was Herod’s lingering regard for John, and that restraint would give way at the first opening. The witnesses see in her the deadly resentment of a guilty conscience that cannot bear reproof, and in John’s peril the danger that ever attends the faithful witness who dares to name the sins of the powerful. Gill explains she ‘lay in wait to take the first opportunity of dispatching him’; the witnesses agree the ‘could not’ marks only a delay, not a deliverance — Herodias’s will to murder was fixed, restrained for a time only by Herod’s lingering regard for John, like a second Jezebel toward a second Elijah. Ellicott renders it ‘had a grudge, or spite, against him,’ and the witnesses agree her settled, persistent hostility waited only for the opportunity that the birthday feast would soon afford.

So the witnesses draw the portrait of guilt that hates its reprover. Herodias could not endure the voice that named her sin, and her conscience, instead of yielding to repentance, hardened into murderous resentment. Yet “she could not” — for, as the next verse shows, Herod’s lingering reverence for John stood for a time between her malice and its object. Where the king wavered, she was fixed; where the Law condemned her, she condemned the prophet; and her patient, watchful hatred would at last find, in a banquet and a dance, the opening it sought. The reprover’s danger, the witnesses observe, lay not in the weak and divided king but in the resolute woman whose sin he had exposed and who would not rest until his voice was stilled.
20:
‘For Herod feared John, knowing that he was a just man and an holy, and observed him… and heard him gladly.’ The witnesses agree this fuller portrait of Herod’s divided heart is peculiar to Mark.

The grammatical-historical commentators unfold it. Ellicott notes the description is ‘peculiar to St. Mark’: ‘just’ marks righteousness toward men, ‘holy’ toward God, and ‘observed’ (better, with the best manuscripts, ‘was much perplexed’) shows a reverence that kept John safe. Robertson traces the imperfects — Herod ‘feared,’ ‘kept him safe,’ ‘was much perplexed,’ ‘heard him gladly’ — the prison interviews bracing ‘his jaded mind… as with a whiff of fresh air,’ even as Herodias plotted.

The Wesleyan Barnes and Wesley press the lesson: Herod ‘did many things’ yet not the one thing required — to put away the adulterous wife — attempting ‘to make a compromise with his conscience’; he ‘heard him gladly,’ a ‘delusive joy’ while ‘Herodias lay in his bosom.’ Gill notes Herod’s reverence and his fear of the multitude alike restrained him.

The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here concerns the baptizing of the nations and so does not bear on the verse. The shared, dominant note is the tragedy of a half-moved conscience: Herod knew John just and holy, feared and protected and gladly heard him, and did many things at his word — yet would not part with his darling sin. The witnesses see in him the perennial type of those who reverence the preacher and relish the sermon while clinging to the very vice it condemns, and whose divided heart leaves them, at the last, the slaves of the lust they will not surrender. Gill notes Herod’s reverence and his fear of the multitude alike restrained him from John’s blood; the witnesses see in him the perennial type of those who reverence the preacher and relish the sermon while clinging to the very vice it condemns, whose divided heart leaves them at the last the slaves of the lust they will not surrender. Robertson traces the imperfect tenses — Herod feared, kept him safe, was much perplexed, heard him gladly — the prison interviews bracing his jaded mind even as Herodias plotted his death.

Barnes presses the tragedy of half-repentance to its point: Herod “attempted to make a compromise with his conscience,” treating John kindly, hearing him gladly, doing many things at his word — yet still clinging to “the wife of his brother.” He did much, but not the one thing required, to break off from his sin. So the witnesses read in him the sad portrait of a man moved but not changed: reverent toward the truth, glad to hear it, and refusing the single demand that would have cost him his darling sin. His religious feeling, real as far as it went, left his heart unmastered; and the same man who kept the prophet safe from Herodias would, when his pride was cornered, deliver him to her at last.
21:
‘And when a convenient day was come, that Herod on his birthday made a supper to his lords.’ The witnesses agree Herodias at last found her opportunity at the birthday banquet.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the feast. Robertson notes the genitive absolute — ‘a day well appointed’ for her purpose, the day she had long awaited — and that the guests were the magnates (megistanes), the military tribunes (chiliarchs), and the chief men of Galilee. Clarke and Barnes identify the ‘lords, high captains, and chief estates’ as the nobles, military commanders, and leading civilians, and note the birthday feast (compare Genesis 40:20).

Gill observes the birthday was kept ‘as a festival, in eating, and drinking, and dancing,’ an ‘opportune and seasonable time’ for Herodias to take advantage of Herod ‘when in a good humour… amidst his company, and in his cups.’ Wesley calls it a day ‘convenient for her purpose.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the long-sought occasion arrived when Herod, on his birthday, feasted the great men of his realm. Herodias, who had bided her time, saw her chance in the festal mirth and the king’s expansive mood. The grammatical commentators stress that the day was ‘well appointed’ for her plot — the wine flowing, the nobles gathered, the king disposed to be generous — so that the very pomp of the banquet became the snare. The witnesses see how times of carnal revelry, when restraint is loosened, are made by malice the occasion for its darkest designs, and how a guilty pair turned a feast into the prelude of a murder. Gill observes the birthday was kept as a festival of eating, drinking, and dancing, an opportune time for Herodias to catch Herod ‘in his cups’; the witnesses agree the long-sought occasion arrived amid the festal mirth, so that the very pomp of the banquet became the snare, times of carnal revelry being made by malice the occasion for its darkest designs. Robertson notes the genitive absolute, ‘a day well appointed’ for her purpose, the guests being the magnates, the military tribunes, and the chief men of Galilee gathered to the feast.

So the witnesses see the trap set within a feast. The birthday banquet (Genesis 40:20 records a like royal feast), with all the pomp of Galilee’s great men — the lords of the court, the captains of the army, the chief men of the province — was the very occasion Herodias had awaited. A public stage where the king’s pride before his guests could be turned against his conscience served her purpose far better than any private hour. The “convenient day” was convenient for evil: what to Herod was a celebration was to his wife an opportunity, carefully chosen, to bind him by the eyes of his nobles to a deed his better feelings would have refused alone. Malice, the witnesses note, is patient, and waits for the hour when circumstance will do its work.
22:
‘And when the daughter of the said Herodias came in, and danced… the king said unto the damsel, Ask of me whatsoever thou wilt.’ The few voices note the shameful dancing and the rash promise it drew.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe it. Robertson notes the textual variants — whether ‘the daughter of Herodias herself’ or, on some manuscripts, Herod’s own daughter also named Herodias — and that, the company having drunk freely, Herodias ‘made her daughter come in and dance,’ stooping to ‘degrade her own daughter like a common’ dancer; such mimetic dancing was ‘an almost unprecedented thing for women of rank’ (compare Isaiah 3:16). Gill names the dancer Salome, daughter not of Herod but of his brother Philip.

Barnes notes simply that she ‘danced’ before them, pleasing Herod and his guests. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: a princess of the royal house was made to perform a lascivious dance before a company of men flushed with wine, and the gratified king, in his folly, bound himself by a rash promise to give her whatever she should ask.

The shared, dominant note is that the dance and the oath are the joint instruments of Herodias’s plot. The mother degraded her daughter’s modesty, and the king, overcome by the spectacle and the heat of wine, threw open his treasury and kingdom to a dancing girl. The witnesses see in the scene the peril of unbridled mirth: pleasure loosens prudence, and a rash vow made to gratify the flesh becomes, in malicious hands, the warrant for a crime — as the next verses will show, when the gift demanded is the head of a prophet. Robertson notes such mimetic dancing was ‘an almost unprecedented thing for women of rank,’ Herodias stooping to degrade her own daughter to carry out her purpose; the witnesses agree the dance and the oath are the joint instruments of her plot, pleasure loosening prudence so that a rash vow made to gratify the flesh becomes, in malicious hands, the warrant for a crime.

So the witnesses see the hinge of the tragedy turn on a dance. A princess of the Herodian house was sent in to dance before a hall of feasting, wine-flushed men — an act beneath her rank — and the spectacle so pleased the king that, in the heat of the moment and the pride of his guests, he flung open his treasury with a reckless oath. What looked like the high point of the feast was the spring of the snare; a mother’s cunning, a daughter’s compliance, and a king’s besotted vanity met in a single moment. Herod, carried away by pleasure and the desire to seem magnificent before his lords, bound himself, before he knew it, to whatever the dancer should demand — and the dancer was her mother’s instrument.
23:
‘And he sware unto her, Whatsoever thou shalt ask of me, I will give it thee, unto the half of my kingdom.’ The few voices note the extravagant, foolish oath.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark its rashness. Robertson notes the ‘swaggering oath to the half of the kingdom’ recalls the same offered to Esther by Ahasuerus (Esther 5:3). Barnes likewise points to Esther (Esther 7:2). Clarke exclaims at ‘a noble price for a dance,’ noting the eastern extravagance toward dancing-girls that has ‘ruined’ men of opulence.

Gill notes Herod ‘added an oath to what he had before said… to confirm it, and to encourage her,’ repeating it in stronger terms; Wesley calls ‘to the half of my kingdom’ a proverbial expression of boundless liberality.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Herod, carried away by the dance and the wine, bound himself with an oath to give the girl whatever she might ask, even to half his kingdom. The grammatical commentators see in the formula the language of royal extravagance — the very words Ahasuerus used to Esther — here squandered on a dancing-girl. The folly of the vow, the witnesses agree, lay both in its boundlessness and in its rashness: a prudent man fears to be snared by the words of his mouth, but Herod, to grace his birthday before his guests, pledged what was not his to give and what he could not honourably perform.

The shared, dominant note is that the oath, sworn to gratify a vain pleasure, became the snare by which a murder was procured. The witnesses see how a promise made in the flush of revelry, and confirmed by a needless oath, delivered the king into the hands of a scheming wife — a warning that rash vows are ensnaring things, and that no oath can bind a man to do what God forbids. Gill notes Herod added the oath ‘to confirm it, and to encourage her,’ repeating it in stronger terms; the witnesses agree the folly of the vow lay both in its boundlessness and its rashness, a prudent man fearing to be snared by his own words, while Herod, to grace his birthday before his guests, pledged what was not his to give and could not honourably perform.

So the witnesses read in Herod’s oath a warning against rash and unconditional promises. To bind oneself by a solemn vow, before witnesses, to grant an unknown request — and that to a girl in the flush of a banquet — was the act of a man whose judgement had drowned in wine and vanity. The proverbial “half of my kingdom,” meant for splendour, proved a snare. A vow sworn without thought becomes a chain; and the honour a man fears to lose by breaking it may bind him to a far greater dishonour in the keeping. The king who would not seem to retract before his lords was about to murder the prophet he revered — his pride of word made the executioner of his conscience.
24:
‘And she went forth, and said unto her mother, What shall I ask? And she said, The head of John the Baptist.’ The few voices agree the daughter consulted her mother, whose long-held malice now spoke.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the consultation. Robertson observes that her going to ask her mother ‘proves that she had not been told beforehand what to ask,’ and that the middle voice (‘what shall I ask for myself?’) shows she was ‘thinking of something for herself’ and ‘unprepared for her mother’s ghastly reply.’ Ellicott notes this consultation is ‘peculiar to St. Mark.’ Gill explains she withdrew to consider and consult, women not sitting at such a feast.

The Reformed Calvin probes Herodias’s motive: not mere revenge, but ‘the dread of being cast off’ that ‘inflamed and tormented her’; she hoped the prophet’s blood would bind Herod to her and secure her power, longing for the death of the one man she saw as her opponent.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the girl, given a blank promise, did not know what to ask and ran to her mother, who seized the moment to demand the head of John. The grammatical commentators stress that the daughter was an instrument, not the author, of the crime; the malice was wholly the mother’s, and Calvin discerns beneath it not simple vengeance but the calculating fear of a woman who would secure her adulterous position by the death of her reprover. The witnesses see in Herodias the deadly resolve of a guilty conscience that will stop at nothing to silence the voice of truth. Ellicott notes the consultation with the mother is peculiar to Mark, and Gill that the girl withdrew to consider, women not sitting at the feast; the witnesses agree the daughter was an instrument, not the author, of the crime, the malice wholly the mother’s, who, as Calvin discerns, acted from the calculating fear of a woman who would secure her adulterous position by the death of her reprover. Robertson notes the middle voice, ‘what shall I ask for myself?’, shows the girl was thinking of something for herself and was unprepared for her mother’s ghastly reply.

So the witnesses watch the trap close. The girl, given a king’s blank promise, runs to her mother for counsel; and the mother, who had waited months for this, names at once the one prize she craved — not gold nor honour, but a man’s head. The horror of the verse lies in its swiftness: a long-nursed hatred needs only an opening, and asks without hesitation for blood. Calvin discerns the root — not bare revenge but the dread of being cast off, the gnawing insecurity of a guilty union, which reckoned the prophet’s death “a great advantage” while his living voice condemned her. The dancing daughter becomes the instrument of the scheming mother, and the feast’s gaiety turns, in a single sentence whispered aside, into the demand for murder.
25:
‘And she came in straightway with haste unto the king… I will that thou give me by and by in a charger the head of John the Baptist.’ The few voices note the eager haste of the demand.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the speed. Robertson notes the ‘straightway with haste’ was ‘before the king’s rash mood passed and while he was still under the spell of the dancing princess’ — ‘Herodias knew her game well.’ Ellicott observes that ‘by and by’ in the old English of the version meant ‘forthwith’ or ‘immediately,’ Mark’s familiar word. Gill notes she returned ‘with much concern, eagerness, and diligence,’ hastened by her mother.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the girl, prompted by her mother, hurried back to press the demand at once, before Herod could recover his sober judgment. The grammatical commentators stress the calculated timing: Herodias understood that the king, flushed with wine and pleasure, might relent if given pause, and so the request was urged ‘immediately’ and with insistence — the head delivered ‘forthwith’ on a charger.

The shared, dominant note is that the murder was rushed through while the snare held. The haste, the witnesses agree, was itself part of the plot: a moment’s delay might have let conscience reassert itself, so Herodias drove her daughter to demand the prophet’s head on the instant. The very eagerness of the request — that the head be brought at once, and served up like a dish at the feast — reveals the depth of the malice that would not rest until the reprover’s voice was silenced for ever. Gill notes she returned ‘with much concern, eagerness, and diligence,’ hastened by her mother; the witnesses agree the murder was rushed through while the snare held, a moment’s delay perhaps letting conscience reassert itself, the very eagerness of the request — the head served up like a dish at the feast — revealing the depth of the malice that would not rest till the reprover was silenced. Ellicott observes that ‘by and by’ in the old English of the version meant ‘forthwith’ or ‘immediately,’ Mark’s familiar word, urging the head be brought on the instant.

So the witnesses see a horror sharpened by haste. The daughter, primed by her mother, does not wait or waver but rushes back while Herod is still warm with wine and pleasure, pressing for immediate execution — “by and by,” on the spot, the head served up like a dish at the feast. The very urgency was part of the cunning: a delay might have let the king’s conscience recover and his reverence for John reassert itself. Not content with the death, the askers would have it instantly and grotesquely, the prophet’s head borne in on a platter into the banqueting hall. Evil, having gained its opening, presses for swift and irrevocable accomplishment before second thoughts can intervene — and Herodias, Robertson says, “knew her game well.”
26:
‘And the king was exceeding sorry; yet for his oath’s sake, and for their sakes which sat with him, he would not reject her.’ The witnesses agree Herod’s grief was real but his cowardice fatal.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the false plea. Robertson notes Herod was ‘caught once again between his conscience and his environment’ — ‘the environment stifled his conscience.’ Clarke observes that the guests ‘probably joined in the request, and were glad of this opportunity to get this light of Israel extinguished.’

The Reformed Calvin presses the analysis: Herod’s heart was ‘no longer influenced by religious sentiments,’ yet, foreseeing the detestation the crime would excite, he repents of his levity — but has not the courage to refuse a dancing girl, ‘as if it were more dishonourable to retract a rash and foolish promise than to persist in a heinous crime.’ Wesley puts it sharply: ‘Herod’s honour was like the conscience of the chief priests’ (Matthew 27:6) — ‘to shed innocent blood wounded neither one nor the other.’

The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced. The shared, dominant note is that no oath can sanctify a sin: Herod, sorry yet unwilling to seem weak before his guests, hid a real cowardice behind a show of scruple for his rash vow. The witnesses agree that a point of honour weighed more with him than a point of conscience, and that he murdered an innocent man rather than break a wicked promise — choosing the greater crime to avoid the lesser shame. His ‘exceeding sorrow,’ the older writers note, condemned rather than excused him, for he grieved at the deed yet did it. Robertson notes Herod was ‘caught once again between his conscience and his environment,’ which stifled it; the witnesses agree that no oath can sanctify a sin, Herod hiding a real cowardice behind a show of scruple for his rash vow, and murdering an innocent man rather than break a wicked promise — choosing the greater crime to avoid the lesser shame, his very sorrow condemning rather than excusing him. Clarke observes the guests probably joined in the request, glad of the opportunity to see this light of Israel extinguished, the reprover of their vices put out of the way.

Calvin lays bare the false shame at the root of the deed: Herod, “foreseeing the detestation” the murder would excite, dreads “the loss of character,” and so repents of his rash oath — “and yet he has not the courage to give a refusal to a dancing girl, lest he should incur the reproach of unsteadiness; as if it were more dishonorable to retract a rash and foolish promise than to persist in a heinous” crime. So the witnesses read in Herod the ruin of a man governed by opinion rather than conscience. He was “exceeding sorry” — his better feeling not dead — but he feared the eyes of his guests and the shame of his word more than he feared God or the guilt of innocent blood. His sorrow, having no courage in it, served only to condemn him.
27:
‘And immediately the king sent an executioner, and commanded his head to be brought.’ The few voices note the swift, secret deed and the Latin word Mark uses for the soldier.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the term. Ellicott and Robertson explain that Mark uses the Latin word speculator — originally a ‘watchman’ or ‘sentinel,’ come to mean a soldier of the bodyguard employed as courier or executioner (so Suetonius and Tacitus); Robertson calls him ‘a spy, scout, lookout, and often executioner,’ one of Herod’s guard. Lightfoot notes the same word transliterated in the Targum. Barnes glosses it ‘one of his guard.’

Gill notes the king sent the executioner ‘immediately,’ the prison being near at hand. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Herod, having yielded, lost no time, dispatching a soldier of his guard to behead John in the prison and bring the head. The Latin term Mark preserves — speculator — is one of the touches that reflect the Roman military setting and, some hold, the Roman audience of his Gospel.

The shared, dominant note is the grim efficiency of the murder. The same haste that marked the demand now marks its execution: the king commands, the guardsman goes, and the prophet is beheaded in the cell where he had so long been held. The witnesses see in the ‘immediately’ both the slave-like subjection of Herod to his fears and his wife, and the cheapness of a holy life in the eyes of a guilty court — a great prophet dispatched, without trial or delay, to gratify the malice of a dancing girl’s mother. Lightfoot notes the Latin word transliterated in the Targum, and Gill that the king sent the executioner immediately, the prison being near; the witnesses agree the same haste that marked the demand now marks its execution, the cheapness of a holy life in a guilty court showing in the prophet dispatched without trial or delay to gratify a dancing girl’s mother. Ellicott explains the word, originally ‘watchman’ or ‘sentinel,’ had come to mean a soldier of the bodyguard employed as courier or executioner, as in Suetonius and Tacitus.

So the witnesses mark the terrible brevity and speed of the deed. The same haste that marked the demand now marks the killing: no trial, no delay, no reprieve — a soldier of the guard (Mark’s Latin speculator) dispatched on the instant from the banqueting hall to the dungeon, to return with a prophet’s head. Barnes notes the word may mean simply “one of his guard,” the officer used to such errands. The long restraint that had guarded John gives way in a moment to a casual, official murder. The greatest of those born of women is put to death not by due process nor for any crime, but at the nod of a wine-heated king, to satisfy a dancer and a vengeful woman — his execution slipped in between the courses of a birthday supper.
28:
‘And brought his head in a charger, and gave it to the damsel: and the damsel gave it to her mother.’ The two voices note the grim trophy passed from hand to hand and the after-fate of Herodias.

The Reformed Gill records simply that the executioner brought the head ‘in a large dish, to Herod,’ whence it passed to the girl and to her mother. Calvin draws the lesson: ‘it was an additional aggravation of this detestable crime, that the head of the holy man was made, after his death, a matter of sport.’ Yet, he adds, in this way the Lord ‘sometimes gives up his people to the pride of wicked men, till he at length makes it evident that their blood is’ precious in his sight (Psalms 116:15).

Calvin marks the providential sequel: Herodias, ‘delighted with the thought of having gained her wicked purpose,’ cruelly triumphed over her reprover, but afterward, ‘stripped of her wealth… deprived of the title of queen… driven from her native country, and destitute,’ dragged out ‘a wretched life in poverty and banishment,’ a spectacle ‘gratifying to angels and to all good people.’

With only two voices the synthesis is short, but they agree on the substance: the head of the greatest of the prophets was carried in on a platter and handed, like a dish at the feast, to the girl and her mother. The witnesses see in this last indignity the full malice of Herodias, who would not be satisfied until she held in triumph the proof of her revenge; and in Calvin’s reading of her later ruin a warning that God may for a time give up his servants to the cruelty of the proud, yet in the end vindicates their precious blood and overturns the wicked in their triumph. Gill records the head was brought ‘in a large dish, to Herod,’ whence it passed to the girl and her mother; the witnesses see in this last indignity the full malice of Herodias, who would not be satisfied until she held in triumph the proof of her revenge, and in Calvin’s reading of her later ruin a warning that God may for a time give up his servants to the proud, yet in the end vindicates their precious blood.

The verse lingers, with awful deliberateness, on the passing of the head from hand to hand — soldier to dancing-girl to scheming mother — as if to fix the horror of it. The prize Herodias had craved is delivered; her long malice is satisfied; the voice that named her sin is silenced and its silencing made a trophy at a feast.
