Yet Calvin warns that her triumph is not what it seems: she “cruelly triumphs over her reprover,” but a worse end awaited her, when, stripped of her wealth, she would share her husband’s ruin. To the eye of the banquet Herodias has won; to the eye of faith a holy man has sealed his fidelity with his blood, and his death is “precious in the sight of the Lord” (Psalm 116:15).
29:
"And when his disciples heard of it, they came and took up his corpse, and laid it in a tomb." The witnesses note the pious courage of John the Baptist's disciples in burying their executed master. Gill identifies them as "the disciples of John," who, hearing of "this barbarous execution," came to perform the last office. Robertson: "It was a mournful time for the disciples of John. They went and told Jesus (Matthew 14:12). What else could they do?"

The Reformed Calvin draws out the meaning of the act. The cruelty of Herodias was almost complete: "It was, to leave the corpse of the holy man unburied; for there is reason to believe that... the attendants of the tyrant had thrown out the corpse." Though "the honor of burial is of no importance to the dead, yet it is the will of the Lord that we should observe this ceremony as a token of the last resurrection; and therefore God was pleased with the carefulness which was manifested by the disciples, when they came to commit to the tomb the body of their master." The burial was, moreover, "an attestation of their piety; for in this way they declared that the doctrine of their master continued to have a firm hold of their hearts after his death." And it was an act of courage: "This confession was therefore worthy of praise, more especially as it was not without danger; for they could not do honor to a man who had been put to death by the executioner without exciting against themselves the rage of the tyrant."

The disciples' going to tell Jesus (Matthew 14:12) the witnesses note as a fitting sequel — the bereaved followers of the forerunner turning to the One whom their master had announced. The death of John and the burial of his body mark the close of his ministry; the voice (Mark 1:3) falls silent, and the disciples are drawn toward the Word.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the account of John's death. The witnesses agree: John's disciples, with pious courage, took up their master's headless body and laid it in a tomb — an act honouring the dead as a token of the resurrection, attesting their continued loyalty to his doctrine, and braving the tyrant's rage. The burial of the forerunner, and the disciples' turning to tell Jesus, mark the handing-on from John to Christ. The faithful burial of the martyr's body, performed at risk, is the seal of a discipleship that death could not shake; and the disciples' recourse to Jesus points to where, in their grief, true comfort and continuance were to be found.
30:
"And the apostles gathered themselves together unto Jesus, and told him all things, both what they had done, and what they had taught." The witnesses note the return of the Twelve from their mission and their report to Christ. Robertson: "Vivid historical present... the constative aorist that summed it all up, the story of this their first tour without Jesus. And Jesus listened to it all (Luke 9:10). He was deeply concerned in the outcome." Ellicott notes the return "is mentioned by St. Luke (Luke 9:10), but not by St. Matthew in this connection." Gill: they "met together in one place, and came in a body together."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers draw out the example. Pseudo-Jerome: "they return to the fountain-head whence the streams flow; those who are sent by God, always offer up thanks for those things which they have received." Theophylact: "Let us also learn, when we are sent on any mission, not to go far away, and not to overstep the bounds of the office committed, but to go often to him who sends us, and report all that we have done and taught; for we must not only teach but act." Bede adds that "His own and John's disciples together tell Him what John had suffered," so that the report of the mission and the news of the Baptist's death came near the same time.

The puritan Henry (covering vv.30-44): the apostles "gathered themselves together, to compare notes, and came to Jesus, the centre of their unity, to give him an account." He draws the ministerial lesson: "Ministers are accountable both for what they do, and for what they teach... Let them not either do any thing, or teach any thing, but what they are willing should be related and repeated to the Lord Jesus." It is a comfort to faithful ministers "when they can appeal to Christ concerning their doctrine and manner of life."

The witnesses agree: the apostles, returning from their first mission, gathered to Christ and reported all they had done and taught — modelling the accountability of those whom God sends, who return to their Master to render account and receive rest. The report of both deeds and words teaches that ministers must watch over their own conduct as well as their doctrine, and that all should be such as they are willing to lay open before the Lord Jesus. The return to Christ, "the centre of their unity," and his attentive listening, display the bond between the Master and his sent ones — a bond to which the weary apostles now return for the rest he will provide (v.31).
31:
"And he said unto them, Come ye yourselves apart into a desert place, and rest a while: for there were many coming and going, and they had no leisure so much as to eat." The witnesses note Christ's tender care for his weary apostles and the lesson of needful rest. Robertson: "It was plain that they were over-wrought and excited and needed refreshment (rest up, literally). This is one of the needed lessons for all preachers and teachers, occasional change and refreshment. Even Jesus felt the need of it." Clarke: "Rest is necessary for those who labor; and a zealous preacher of the Gospel will as often stand in need of it as a galley slave." Barnes notes the cause: "they were thronged, that there was a vast multitude attending his preaching."

The puritan Henry (under v.30) drew out the kindness: "He said unto them, perceiving them to be almost spent, and out of breath, Come ye yourselves apart into a desert place, and rest awhile... With what kindness and compassion doth Christ say to them, Come, and rest!" He notes the qualifications: they are to come "themselves apart" — "if they must rest, they must be alone"; not to a pleasant country-seat but "into a desert place"; and only "to rest awhile... There is no remaining rest for the people of God till they come to heaven." The reason was the constant hurry: "they were in a constant hurry, so that they had not their work in any order."

Theophylact (under v.30) drew the lesson for leaders: "Christ makes His disciples rest, that men who are set over others may learn, that they who labour in any work or in the word deserve rest, and ought not to labour continually."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ, perceiving his apostles worn out by the constant press of the crowd, tenderly called them to come apart and rest — teaching that even the most active servants of Christ need seasons of refreshment, and that the Lord considers the frame of their bodies. The call to a desert place, alone, for a little while, models the wisdom of withdrawal for rest; yet it is rest "awhile" only, for the full rest remains in heaven. The lesson is for all who labour in Christ's work: needful change and refreshment are not idleness but the provision of a compassionate Master, who himself felt the need of it and made room for it. The withdrawal sought, however, would be interrupted by the crowd's pursuit (vv.32-34), turning the place of rest into the scene of the feeding.
32:
"And they departed into a desert place by ship privately." The brief verse records the withdrawal of Christ and his apostles for rest. Gill notes the place "belonged to the city of Bethsaida." Barnes: "By a boat, a small vessel. Privately — without making their plan known. They intended to go privately. It appears, however, that their intention became known, and multitudes followed them." Robertson: "They accepted with alacrity and off they went." Wesley: "Across a creek or corner of the lake."

The withdrawal by ship, the witnesses note, was the means of the rest Christ had commanded (v.31). Henry (under v.30): "They withdrew, accordingly, by ship; not crossing the water, but making a coasting voyage to the desert of Bethsaida. Going by water was much less toilsome than going by land would have been. They went away privately, that they might be by themselves. The most public persons cannot but wish to be private sometimes."

The intended privacy was soon broken by the crowd (v.33), yet the attempt at retirement shows the genuine need for rest and the wisdom of seeking it. Christ and his apostles sought a quiet place, away from the press, to recover their strength; but the eagerness of the people would follow them even there. The desert place near Bethsaida, chosen for solitude, would become the scene of the feeding of the five thousand — the rest sought turned into a fresh occasion of ministry.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the departure for rest. The witnesses agree on its sense: Christ and the Twelve withdrew by ship to a desert place, seeking the privacy and rest he had enjoined. The voyage by water spared them the toil of the road; the attempt at retirement honoured the genuine need for refreshment. Yet, as the next verse shows, the crowds would not be left behind, and the sought-for solitude would yield to the pressing needs of the multitude. The verse thus marks both the wisdom of seeking rest and the readiness of Christ to lay it aside when compassion called — for when the people followed, he met them not with displeasure but with teaching and bread (vv.34-44). The private withdrawal becomes the setting for one of the greatest of the miracles.
33:
"And the people saw them departing, and many knew him, and ran afoot thither out of all cities, and outwent them, and came together unto him." The witnesses note the eager pursuit of the crowds, who outran the boat on foot. Gill: "the multitude that came to and fro, saw Christ and his disciples taking ship... for though they were as private as could be, and intended to have gone in a very private manner, yet the people observed them." Ellicott marks the contrast: "The words are used to point the contrast between the disciples, who had come in their boat, and the crowds who came by land." Robertson: "The crowds were not to be outdone. They recognized Jesus and the disciples and ran around the head of the lake on foot and got there ahead of Jesus and were waiting for Him when the boat came." Clarke notes the verse "has suffered so much from transcribers," with many variants.

The people's eager pursuit the witnesses commend. Henry (under v.30): "It was rude to do so, when he and his disciples were desirous... to retire; and yet they are not blamed for it, nor bid to go back, but bid welcome. A failure in good manners will easily be excused in those who follow Christ, if it be but made up in a fulness of good affections." He marvels at their zeal: "Here is no time set, no meeting appointed, no bell tolled; yet they thus fly like a cloud, and as the doves to their windows. They followed him out of the cities, quitted their houses and shops... They ran afoot, and made such haste, that they out-went the disciples."

Bede (under v.30) read their coming as a trial of faith: "Here He tries the faith of the multitude, and by seeking a desert place He would see whether they care to follow Him. And they follow Him, and not on horseback, nor in carriages, but laboriously coming on foot, they shew how great is their anxiety for their salvation."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the crowds, seeing Christ depart and recognizing him, ran on foot from all the cities and outran the boat, gathering to him before he landed. Their eager, breathless pursuit — leaving houses, shops, and callings to follow him into a desert place — displays the hunger of the people for Christ and his word, a zeal that excused their intrusion on his sought-for rest. The trial of faith (Bede) was met: they cared enough to follow on foot. Their pursuit turned the place of intended rest into the scene of teaching and feeding, for Christ, far from sending them away, met their eagerness with compassion (v.34). The hungry crowds running to Christ are, for the witnesses, a rebuke to our own coldness and a pattern of holy eagerness for the bread of life.
34:
"And Jesus, when he came out, saw much people, and was moved with compassion toward them, because they were as sheep not having a shepherd: and he began to teach them many things." The witnesses note Christ's compassion and the pathos of a leaderless people. Barnes: "They had no one to teach them, and guide them. The priests and scribes were proud, corrupt, and despised the common people and neglected them." Robertson: "Matthew has these words in another context (Matthew 9:36), but Mark alone has them here... The people had plenty of official leaders but these rabbis were for spiritual matters blind leaders of the blind. Jesus had come over for rest, but his heart was touched by the pathos of this situation. So he began to teach them many things — and he kept it up."

The Antiochene Theophylact (under v.30) drew out the contrast with the false shepherds: "The Pharisees being ravening wolves did not feed the sheep, but devoured them; for which reason they gather themselves to Christ, the true Shepherd, who gave them spiritual food, that is, the word of God." Seeing them "tired from the length of the way, He pitied them, and wished to satisfy their wish by teaching them."

The puritan Henry (under v.30) marked the graciousness: "instead of being moved with displeasure, because they disturbed him when he desired to be private, as many a good man would have been, he was moved with compassion toward them, and looked upon them with concern, because they were as sheep having no shepherd; they seemed to be well-inclined, and manageable as sheep, and willing to be taught, but they had no shepherd... and therefore, in compassion to them, he not only healed their sick, but he taught them many things."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ, instead of resenting the crowd that broke his rest, was moved with compassion at the sight of a people leaderless and untaught — like sheep without a shepherd, neglected by the proud scribes and devoured by the Pharisaic wolves — and he began to teach them many things. His compassion overcame his own need for rest; his concern was for the spiritual hunger of the multitude, whom he fed first with the word of God and then with bread (vv.35-44). The image of the shepherdless sheep (Numbers 27:17; 1 Kings 22:17) reveals both the misery of the people under their false guides and the heart of the true Shepherd, who came to seek, feed, and guide them. The teaching precedes the feeding: the bread of the word before the bread of the body.
35:
"And when the day was now far spent, his disciples came unto him, and said, This is a desert place, and now the time is far passed." The witnesses note the disciples' concern for the crowd and the setting of the great miracle. Robertson explains the time: "Much day-time already gone. It was after 3 P.M., the first evening." Bede: "The time being far spent, points out that it was evening... By these words He calls on His Apostles, to break bread for the people, that they might be able to testify that they had no bread, and thus the greatness of the miracle might become more known."

The Antiochene and Latin fathers read the whole feeding richly. Theophylact commends the disciples' growing love: "See now, how those who are disciples of Christ grow in love to man, for they pity the multitudes, and come to Christ to intercede for them. But the Lord tried them, to see whether they would know that His power was great enough to feed them." On Christ's looking up to heaven, the Chrysostom material: "before He performed the miracle, He referred to His Father... He also looks up to heaven, that He may teach us to seek our food from God." On the twelve baskets: "It was a proof of overflowing power not only to feed so many men, but also to leave such a superabundance of fragments... Jesus, being the Lord, makes His gifts with superabundant profusion."

Bede gives the great typology: "By the five loaves are figured the Five Books of Moses, by the two fishes, the Psalms and Prophets... He breaks and distributes to the disciples that they may place the bread before the multitudes, because He has opened the mysteries of prophecy to holy doctors, who are to preach them to the whole world." The twelve baskets typify "the Apostles and the following Doctors... externally despised by men, but inwardly full of healthful food." Gregory reads the ranks of fifties and hundreds as "the divers churches which make up the one Catholic."

The witnesses agree: as evening drew on in the desert place, the disciples, pitying the hungry crowd, came to Christ — and he would use the occasion to try their faith and display his power. The fathers read the five loaves and two fishes as the Law and the Prophets, opened by Christ and distributed through the apostles to the world, with the twelve baskets of fragments as the abundant store the Church's teachers carry. The setting — a desert place, the day far spent, a vast and hungry multitude, and only a little food — magnifies the miracle to come, in which Christ proves himself the great Feeder who satisfies all who come to him.
36:
"Send them away, that they may go into the country round about, and into the villages, and buy themselves bread: for they have nothing to eat." The brief verse records the disciples' proposal to dismiss the hungry crowd. Gill: "Leave off preaching, dismiss the assembly." Ellicott explains the place: "Better, the farms — i.e., the enclosed, cultivated land." Robertson: "The fields were the scattered farms. The villages may have included Bethsaida Julias not far away."

The disciples' suggestion sprang from a real but limited compassion. Henry (under v.30): "the disciples thought it would be a kindness to them to dismiss them." They saw the people's need but supposed the only remedy was to send them away to buy food — not yet grasping that Christ could feed them where they were. Theophylact (under v.35) had noted both their love and their limited faith: they "pity the multitudes, and come to Christ to intercede for them," yet did not know "that His power was great enough to feed them."

A telling detail, the witnesses note, is that the crowd themselves did not ask to be sent away. Henry: "we do not find that the multitude themselves did. They did not say, Send us away (though they could not but be hungry), for they esteemed the words of Christ's mouth more than their necessary food, and forgot themselves when they were hearing him." The people's hunger for the word made them forget their hunger for bread; it was the disciples who, out of concern, proposed dismissal.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the disciples' proposal. The witnesses agree: the disciples, moved by a genuine but limited compassion, asked Christ to send the crowd away to buy food in the surrounding farms and villages — not yet understanding that he who had taught them could also feed them. Their suggestion, kind in intent, fell short of faith; it would be answered not by dismissal but by the command "Give ye them to eat" (v.37), which would draw out their faith and prepare for the miracle. The contrast between the disciples' anxious proposal and the crowd's contented hunger for the word is instructive: the people prized Christ's teaching above their bread, while the disciples thought first of their bodily need. Christ would meet both, feeding the multitude where they sat, that none should be sent away empty.
37:
"He answered and said unto them, Give ye them to eat. And they say unto him, Shall we go and buy two hundred pennyworth of bread, and give them to eat?" The witnesses note Christ's testing command and the disciples' incredulous reply. Gill: "This he said to try their faith, and make way for the following miracle." Lightfoot, with rabbinic learning, explains the sum "two hundred pence" (denarii) as "a sum very famous, and of very frequent mention" among the Jews — the standard portion in various legal cases — which is perhaps why it came so readily to the disciples' lips. Barnes reckons it "about 27 dollars, or £6 sterling," and notes that, since the disciples "had a common purse," this may have been about all they possessed. He identifies the speaker (from John 6:7) as Philip, who asked "with a mixture of wonder and agitation, whether they should take all their little property, and spend it on a single meal."

The command "Give ye them to eat" the witnesses read as a deliberate test. Henry (under v.30): "Christ would let them see their folly in forecasting for themselves, that they might put the greater value upon his provision for them." Theophylact (under v.35): "the disciples thought that He did not know what was necessary for the feeding of so large a multitude, for their answer shews that they were troubled."

The disciples' reply reveals the weakness of their faith. Henry: "through the weakness of their faith, instead of waiting for directions from Christ, they perplex the cause with projects of their own." Their objection echoes Moses: "Shall the flocks and the herds be slain for them?" (Numbers 11:22). They reckoned the cost and found it impossible, not yet looking to the power of Christ.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ met the disciples' proposal to dismiss the crowd with the testing command "Give ye them to eat" — designed to try their faith and prepare for the miracle. Their incredulous reply, reckoning the impossible cost of two hundred pennyworth (likely their whole purse), exposed the weakness of their faith, which calculated their own resources instead of waiting on Christ's power. The command laid on them a duty beyond their means, that they might learn to look to him for the supply; and their failure to do so, like Moses' "Shall the flocks be slain?" (Numbers 11:22), sets off the more brightly the provision Christ was about to make. The lesson is that Christ may bid his servants do what they cannot, to teach them dependence on the power that works through them.
38:
"He saith unto them, How many loaves have ye? go and see. And when they knew, they say, Five, and two fishes." The brief verse records Christ's question and the slender store of food. Gill: "This he said, not as ignorant, but as willing to try their faith yet more, and that the after miracle might be more manifest and illustrious." Robertson explains the harmony with John's fuller account: "John says that Jesus asked Philip to find out what food they had... Then Andrew reports the fact of the lad with five barley loaves and two fishes (John 6:8f). They had suggested before that two hundred pennyworth was wholly inadequate... John's Gospel alone tells of the lad with his lunch which his mother had given him."

Christ's question "How many loaves have ye? go and see" the witnesses read as a further trial of faith and a setting of the stage for the miracle. By having the disciples ascertain and report the tiny store — five loaves and two fishes — Christ ensured that the greatness of the miracle would be plain and undeniable. Bede (under v.35) had noted that Christ called the apostles to break bread "that they might be able to testify that they had no bread, and thus the greatness of the miracle might become more known."

The five loaves and two fishes — barley loaves, the food of the poor, brought by a lad (John 6:9) — were a slender provision indeed for so vast a multitude. Henry (under v.30) noted they were "brought with them for the victualling of their ship," and "this was but a little for Christ and his disciples, and yet this they must give away." The smallness of the store both magnifies the miracle and teaches that Christ can multiply the little we bring him.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ's question drew from the disciples the report of their slender store — five loaves and two fishes — both to try their faith further and to make the coming miracle undeniable. By establishing how little there was, Christ ensured that all would know the multiplication was his work alone. The five barley loaves and two fishes, a poor lad's lunch, are a figure of the little we bring to Christ, which in his hands becomes more than enough. The verse, with John's detail of the lad, shows the harmony of the eyewitness accounts and prepares for the miracle in which Christ, taking the small provision, would feed five thousand and leave twelve baskets over. What is little in our hands is much in his.
39:
"And he commanded them to make all sit down by companies upon the green grass." The witnesses note the orderly arrangement of the multitude and the vivid detail of the green grass. Gill: Christ "directed them to place them" in order, "as they used to do at table when about to take a meal." Ellicott marks the distributive force: "The Greek expresses the distributive force of the English by simple repetition, companies and companies." He notes "the green grass may be noted as an example of St. Mark's vividness, and serves as an indirect note of time pointing to the same season as that specified by St. John, sc., a little before the Passover (John 6:10)." Robertson: "It was passover time and the afternoon sun shone upon the orderly groups upon the green spring grass."

The orderly arrangement the witnesses read as fitting the God of order. Henry (under v.30): "the guests were orderly; for they sat down by companies on the green grass, they sat down in ranks by hundreds and by fifties, that the provision might the more easily and regularly be distributed among them; for God is the God of order, and not of confusion. Thus care was taken that every one should have enough, and none be over-looked."

The detail "green grass" the witnesses (with Robertson) note as a mark of the season — the spring, near Passover (John 6:4) — and of Mark's eyewitness vividness, drawn from Peter's memory. The grass is green in Palestine "mainly at the passover time," giving an incidental indication of the chronology of Christ's ministry.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ commanded the multitude to be seated in orderly companies on the green grass — an arrangement fitting the God of order, ensuring that the food could be distributed regularly and none overlooked. The vivid detail of the green grass, peculiar to Mark and drawn from Peter's recollection, marks the season as spring, near Passover, and stands as a small but telling touch of eyewitness accuracy. The orderly seating, by hundreds and fifties (v.40), prepared for the smooth distribution of the miraculous provision, so that the vast crowd was fed without confusion. The scene — rows of people in their many-coloured garments seated on the spring grass — is one of the Gospel's most vivid pictures, and a reminder that Christ's provision is dispensed not in chaos but in order, that every one may be supplied.
40:
"And they sat down in ranks, by hundreds, and by fifties." The witnesses note the precise, garden-like arrangement of the multitude. Gill explains the word "ranks": "Or beds: in such form as little beds are placed in a garden, or as rows of vines in a vineyard, in which form the scholars of the wise men sat in their schools." Lightfoot confirms from the Talmud: "the university of Jabneh is very frequently celebrated under the name of the vineyard in Jabneh... Because the scholars sat there ranks by ranks, like a vineyard which is planted rank by rank." Ellicott: "The primary meaning of the Greek word is a bed of flowers or herbs." Robertson paints the scene: "They were arranged in groups by hundreds and by fifties and they looked like garden beds with their many-coloured clothes... Garden beds, garden beds. Peter saw and he never forgot the picture and so Mark caught it. There was colour as well as order in the grouping."

The arrangement by hundreds and fifties the witnesses note as both orderly and practical. Wesley explains the reckoning: "fifty in rank, and a hundred in file. So a hundred multiplied by fifty, make just five thousand." Barnes cautions against over-precision: "We need not suppose that these were exactly formed... but that this was about the number." The grouping facilitated the orderly distribution of the food, so that none was overlooked.

Robertson draws the chronological point (with Gould): "The grass is not green in Palestine much of the year, mainly at the passover time. So here the Synoptic Gospels have an indication of more than a one-year ministry of Jesus." The green grass and the orderly ranks together fix the scene at Passover, about a year before the crucifixion.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the multitude sat in orderly ranks, by hundreds and fifties, like garden beds or the rows of a vineyard — a vivid picture, drawn from Peter's memory, of colour and order combined. The arrangement, recalling the seating of scholars in the rabbinic schools, both displayed the God of order and made possible the smooth distribution of the miraculous food among five thousand. The scene of the many-coloured groups seated on the green spring grass is one of Mark's most memorable touches, and an incidental witness to the Passover season and the length of Christ's ministry. The careful ordering of the crowd prepared for the careful feeding, that every one of the great multitude should be filled (v.42).
41:
"And when he had taken the five loaves and the two fishes, he looked up to heaven, and blessed, and brake the loaves, and gave them to his disciples to set before them; and the two fishes divided he among them all." The witnesses note the manner of the miracle — the looking up, the blessing, the breaking, the distribution through the disciples. Gill: Christ took the loaves and fishes "out of the hands of those that brought them into his own." Clarke discusses the blessing: "the word God should be inserted here... The food we receive from God is already blessed... but God, who gives it, deserves our warmest thanksgivings." Robertson notes the fishes "were in excess of the twelve baskets full of broken pieces of bread."

The looking up to heaven the fathers (under v.35) read richly. The Chrysostom material: "before He performed the miracle, He referred to His Father... He also looks up to heaven, that He may teach us to seek our food from God, and not from the devil... By this also He intimated to the crowd, that He could not be opposed to God, since He called upon God." Bede: "He looks up to heaven, that He may teach us that there we must look for grace. He breaks and distributes to the disciples that they may place the bread before the multitudes, because He has opened the mysteries of prophecy to holy doctors, who are to preach them to the whole world."

The puritan Henry (under v.30) marked that "Christ did not call one of his disciples to crave a blessing, but did it himself; and by virtue of this blessing the bread strangely multiplied." The distribution through the disciples, the fathers note, was so "that by handling the bread, they might see that it was an undoubted miracle" (Theophylact, under v.35).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ took the slender store, looked up to heaven, blessed and broke it, and gave it to the disciples to distribute — and in his hands and theirs it multiplied to feed the multitude. The looking up taught the people and us to seek our food from God, and showed Christ in harmony with the Father; the blessing was the means by which the bread strangely multiplied; the distribution through the disciples both made them witnesses of the miracle by handling the bread and figured (the fathers add) the apostles' office of dispensing to the world the mysteries Christ has opened. The whole manner of the miracle — gratitude to the Father, the multiplying blessing, the orderly distribution — displays Christ as the great Feeder who, looking to heaven, supplies the needs of all who come to him.
42:
"And they did all eat, and were filled." The briefest verse of this section — with only Gill and Barnes in the Catena — records the completeness of the miraculous provision. Gill cross-references the parallel. Barnes points to Deuteronomy 8:3, "man doth not live by bread only, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of the Lord doth man live" — joining the bodily feeding to the deeper truth of God's provision.

The two facts of the verse — "they did all eat, and were filled" — the witnesses note as the proof and measure of the miracle. All ate: the provision reached every one of the five thousand, none overlooked. And all were filled: not merely tasted, but satisfied. Henry (under v.30) drew out the abundance: "they did all eat, and were filled, though they were to the number of five thousand... This miracle was significant, and shows that Christ came into the world, to be the great feeder as well as the great healer; not only to restore, but to preserve and nourish, spiritual life; and in him there is enough for all that come to him, enough to fill the soul, to fill the treasures; none are sent empty away from Christ, but those that come to him full of themselves."

The completeness of the satisfaction — all filled from five loaves and two fishes — magnifies the power of Christ. The fathers (under v.35) had noted that, unlike Moses' manna (measured to each one's need, with the excess breeding worms), "Jesus, being the Lord, makes His gifts with superabundant profusion." Here all are filled, and twelve baskets remain over (v.43).

The thin comment matches the shortest verse in the section. The witnesses agree: the whole multitude ate and were filled — the proof and measure of the miracle. That all ate shows the provision reached every one; that all were filled shows it satisfied, not merely tasted. From five loaves and two fishes, five thousand were fully fed, displaying Christ as the great Feeder in whom there is enough for all who come — enough to satisfy the soul as well as the body. The verse, joined by Barnes to Deuteronomy 8:3, points beyond the bodily bread to the deeper feeding of the word and of Christ himself, the bread of life (John 6), of whom the multiplied loaves were a sign. None who come to Christ are sent away empty; all who come are filled. The satisfied multitude is a figure of the soul that feeds on Christ and wants no good thing.
43:
"And they took up twelve baskets full of the fragments, and of the fishes." The witnesses note the gathering of the fragments and its lesson. Gill: "Of the broken pieces of bread, after all had eaten, and were satisfied." Barnes: "Baskets belonging to the disciples, in which they carried their provisions, or, perhaps, belonging to some of the multitude." Clarke: "either the baskets used by the disciples, or baskets belonging to some of the multitude, who might have brought some with them to carry provisions."

The twelve baskets of fragments the witnesses read as a proof of the miracle's abundance and a lesson against waste. Henry (under v.30): "Though Christ had bread enough at command, he would hereby teach us, not to make waste of any of God's good creatures; remembering how many there are that do want, and that we know not but we may some time or other want such fragments as we throw away."

The fathers (under v.35) drew out the typology and the proof of overflowing power. The Chrysostom material: "Twelve baskets of fragments remained over and above, that each of the Apostles, carrying a basket on his shoulder, might recognise the unspeakable wonder of the miracle. For it was a proof of overflowing power not only to feed so many men, but also to leave such a superabundance of fragments." Unlike Moses' manna, which bred worms when kept, or Elijah's provision measured to need, "Jesus, being the Lord, makes His gifts with superabundant profusion." Bede saw in the twelve baskets "the Apostles and the following Doctors... externally despised by men, but inwardly full of healthful food."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the gathering of twelve baskets of fragments — more than the original store — displayed the superabundant power of Christ, who not only fed five thousand from five loaves but left a surplus far exceeding the beginning. The detail teaches against waste of God's good gifts, mindful of those in want; and it served as a proof of the miracle, each apostle bearing a basket as witness. The fathers read the twelve baskets as a figure of the apostles and the Church's teachers, outwardly lowly yet inwardly full of the healthful food of the word. The overflowing surplus marks the difference between the measured provision of Moses and Elijah and the lavish bounty of Christ the Lord, whose gifts exceed all need. What was little in the hand became more than enough, with twelve baskets to spare.
44:
"And they that did eat of the loaves were about five thousand men." The witnesses note the great number fed and the force of the miracle as proof of Christ's divinity. Gill: "And also of the fishes; for they all ate of both." Ellicott marks the precision: "St. Mark uses the word which excludes women and children" — so the five thousand were men only, the total far greater. Clarke: "This miracle is mentioned by all the four evangelists. It is one of the most astonishing that Christ has wrought. It is a miracle which could not be counterfeited, and a full proof of the divinity of Christ."

Robertson stresses the weight of the evidence: "Men as different from women as in Matthew 14:21. This remarkable miracle is recorded by all Four Gospels, a nature miracle that only God can work. No talk about accelerating natural processes will explain this miracle. And three eyewitnesses report it: the Logia of Matthew, the eyes of Peter in Mark, the witness of John the Beloved Disciple. The evidence is overwhelming."

The number — five thousand men, besides women and children — measures the greatness of the miracle. From five loaves and two fishes, a multitude that would fill a small city was fed and satisfied, with twelve baskets over. The feeding of the five thousand is the only miracle (apart from the resurrection) recorded in all four Gospels, a fact the witnesses note as a mark of its importance and its overwhelming attestation.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which gives the number fed. The witnesses agree: the five thousand men (besides women and children) fed from five loaves and two fishes magnify the miracle to the utmost — a nature miracle that only God can work, recorded by all four Evangelists, and attested by three eyewitnesses (Matthew, Peter through Mark, and John). The vast number, the slender store, and the overwhelming testimony together make the feeding a full proof of Christ's divinity, a miracle that "could not be counterfeited" (Clarke). The verse closes the account of the feeding with the measure of its greatness, and prepares for the next display of Christ's power over nature — his walking on the sea (vv.45-52) — which the disciples, had they understood the loaves, would have received without amazement (v.52). The great Feeder is shown to be the Lord of creation.
45:
"And straightway he constrained his disciples to get into the ship, and to go to the other side before unto Bethsaida, while he sent away the people." The witnesses note the urgency of the dispersal and the reason behind it. Robertson explains: "The crowds had become so excited that they were in the mood to start a revolution against the Roman government and proclaim Jesus king (John 6:15)... He had already forced in reality the disciples to leave in a boat... in order to get them out of this atmosphere of overwrought excitement with a political twist to the whole conception of the Messianic Kingdom." Wesley notes the disciples "did not care to go without him." On the place, Ellicott and Clarke address the Bethsaida question: there were two Bethsaidas, and the boat was to go to the western one (or toward Capernaum, John 6:17).

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the walking on the water that follows. The Gloss: "by the miracle of the loaves He shewed that He is the Creator of the world: but now by walking on the waves He proved that He had a body free from the weight of all sin, and by appeasing the winds... He declared Himself to be the Master of the elements." Bede defends the orthodox faith against the heretic Theodore of Pharan, who "wrote that the Lord had no bodily weight in His flesh, and walked on the sea without weight; but the Catholic faith declares that He had weight according to the flesh." Bede gives the great mystical reading: "the toil of the disciples in rowing, and the contrary wind, mark out the labours of the Holy Church, who amidst the beating waves of the world... strives to reach the repose of her celestial country... sometimes the Church is afflicted by a pressure from the Gentiles so overwhelming, that her Redeemer seems to have entirely deserted her. But the Lord sees His own, toiling on the sea."

The puritan Henry (covering vv.45-56): Christ "departed into a mountain, to pray... he prayed alone, and, for want of a closet, went up into a mountain." On the storm: "The church is often like a ship at sea, tossed with tempests, and not comforted; we may have Christ for us, and yet wind and tide against us; but it is a comfort to Christ's disciples in a storm, that their Master is in the heavenly mount, interceding for them." On his coming: "If Christ's visits to his people be deferred long, yet at length he will come; and their extremity is his opportunity." On "It is I": "The knowledge of Christ, as he is in himself, and near to us, is enough to make the disciples of Christ cheerful even in a storm."

The witnesses agree: Christ urgently sent the disciples ahead by boat and dismissed the crowd, to defuse the dangerous excitement that would have made him a political king, and withdrew to the mountain to pray. The whole episode — the toiling disciples, the contrary wind, Christ's coming on the water — is read by the fathers as a figure of the Church tossed in the world's storms, seemingly deserted yet watched and rescued by her Lord, who is Master of the elements and very God in true human flesh.
46:
"And when he had sent them away, he departed into a mountain to pray." The brief verse records Christ's withdrawal to the mountain for prayer. Gill: "Either his disciples, or rather the multitude" were sent away. Barnes cross-references Christ's habit of solitary prayer (Matthew 6:6; Mark 1:35; Luke 6:12).

The withdrawal to pray, after the crowd was dismissed and the disciples sent ahead, the witnesses read as a model of secret devotion. Henry (under v.45): "He prayed; though he had so much preaching-work upon his hands, yet he was much in prayer; he prayed often, and prayed long, which is an encouragement to us to depend upon the intercession he is making for us at the right hand of the Father." And: "He went alone, to pray; though he needed not to retire for the avoiding either of distraction or of ostentation, yet, to set us an example... he prayed alone, and, for want of a closet, went up into a mountain, to pray. A good man is never less alone than when alone with God."

John (6:15) gives the immediate occasion: Christ withdrew to the mountain "himself alone" because the crowd "would come and take him by force, to make him a king." The prayer follows both the dismissal of the political excitement and the great labour of teaching and feeding — Christ retiring to commune with the Father before the night's work of coming to his disciples on the sea. Bede (under v.45) noted: "Not every man who prays goes up into a mountain, but he alone prays well, who seeks God in prayer."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: having sent away the crowd and the disciples, Christ withdrew to the mountain to pray alone — a model of secret, earnest devotion. Though sinless and under no need to avoid distraction or display, he prayed alone to set us an example and to encourage our dependence on the intercession he ever makes for us. The mountain prayer, following the feeding and the defusing of the crowd's dangerous zeal, shows Christ seeking the Father before the next labour of love. His example teaches that the most active servants must yet find time to be alone with God; and his interceding presence on the mountain, while the disciples toiled on the sea (v.48), is read (with Henry) as a comfort to the storm-tossed Church, whose Master pleads for her from the heavenly height. The praying Christ on the mountain is the type of the interceding Christ at the Father's right hand.
47:
"And when even was come, the ship was in the midst of the sea, and he alone on the land." The witnesses note the second evening and the separation of Christ from his toiling disciples. Gill: "the second evening, and it was properly night." Robertson: "The second or late evening, six P.M. at this season, or sunset on... Jesus had come down out of the mountain where he had prayed to the Father. He is by the sea again in the late twilight. Apparently Jesus remained quite a while, some hours, on the beach. It was now dark and Jesus had not yet come to them (John 6:17)."

The scene — the ship in the midst of the sea, Christ alone on the land — the fathers read mystically. Bede (under v.45): "And well is it said that the ship was in the midst of the sea, and He alone on land, for sometimes the Church is afflicted by a pressure from the Gentiles so overwhelming, that her Redeemer seems to have entirely deserted her. But the Lord sees His own, toiling on the sea, for, lest they faint in tribulations, He strengthens them by the look of His love."

The detail "he alone on the land" the witnesses note as another of Mark's vivid, eyewitness touches (drawn from Peter). Christ, having prayed on the mountain, came down to the shore and watched his disciples struggling on the water — present to them in his gaze even before he came to them on the waves. The separation, with the disciples toiling against the wind and Christ alone on the land, sets the stage for the miracle of his coming to them (v.48).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: as night fell, the ship was in the midst of the sea and Christ alone on the land — a vivid, eyewitness picture of the separation between the toiling disciples and their absent Lord. The fathers read it as a figure of the Church, seemingly deserted in her tribulations, yet watched over by her Redeemer, who sees his own struggling and strengthens them by the look of his love. Christ, having prayed on the mountain, was not indifferent to his disciples' plight but observed them from the shore, present in his gaze before he came in person. The scene of the lone ship and the watching Lord prepares for his coming on the water — the seeming absence turned to a saving presence. Even when Christ appears far off, he sees and will come; the disciples were never out of his sight, nor is the storm-tossed Church out of his.
48:
"And he saw them toiling in rowing; for the wind was contrary unto them: and about the fourth watch of the night he cometh unto them, walking upon the sea, and would have passed by them." The witnesses note Christ's seeing, his timing, his walking, and the strange "would have passed by them." Gill: "He saw them either with his bodily eyes from the mountain... or he perceived in his Spirit, he knew, by virtue of his omniscience as God, what distress his disciples were in." Robertson explains the terms: "βασανιζομενους, to torture, torment... distress as here. ἐλαύνειν is literally to drive as of ships... About the fourth watch of the night, that is, between three and six A.M. The wind was contrary... a great wind (John 6:18), and as a result the disciples had made little progress." On the puzzling clause: "And he would have passed by them. Only in Mark. He wished to pass by them... He wished to pass by them, walking, as if he was passing by" (Wesley).

The puritan Henry (under v.45) unfolds each point. On the timing: "He did not come till the fourth watch of the night, not till after three o'clock in the morning; but then he came. Note, If Christ's visits to his people be deferred long, yet at length he will come; and their extremity is his opportunity." On the walking: "though the floods lift up their voice, the Lord on high is mightier (Psalm 93:3,4). No difficulties can obstruct Christ's gracious appearances for his people." On "would have passed by them": "he set his face and steered his course, as if he would have gone further... this he did, to awaken them to call to him... Providence, when it is acting designedly for the succour of God's people, yet sometimes seems as if it were giving them the go-by."

Augustine (under v.45) gave the same purpose: "His wish to pass them would wring from them that cry, which called for His help." Theophylact (under v.45): "the Lord permitted His disciples to be in danger, that they might learn patience... He did not immediately come to their aid, but allowed them to remain in danger all night, that He might teach them to wait patiently."

The witnesses agree: Christ saw his disciples toiling against the contrary wind, came to them in the fourth watch walking on the sea, and seemed about to pass them by — a delay and a feint designed to try their patience and to draw from them the cry for help. His coming at the night's last watch teaches that, though his help may tarry, he comes in their extremity; his walking on the storm-tossed sea proclaims him Lord over the elements; and his seeming to pass by, like providence that appears to give the go-by, was meant to awaken their prayer. The whole scene displays a Saviour who sees his struggling people, comes at the right time, and tests their faith only to strengthen it.
49:
"But when they saw him walking upon the sea, they supposed it had been a spirit, and cried out." The brief verse records the disciples' terror at the sight of Christ on the water. Gill cross-references the parallel. Barnes points to Job 9:8 ("[God] alone... treadeth upon the waves of the sea") and Luke 24:37 (the disciples' later fear that the risen Christ was a spirit). Clarke: "They supposed it had been a spirit — that is, by whom the storm had been raised."

The disciples' mistaking Christ for a spirit the witnesses read as the natural reaction of frightened men in the dark. Henry (under v.45): "They were frightened at the sight of him, supposing him to have been an apparition; They all saw him, and were troubled, thinking it had been some daemon, or evil genius, that haunted them, and raised this storm. We often perplex and frighten ourselves with phantasms, the creatures of our own fancy and imagination."

Augustine (under v.45) had noted the cause of their failure to recognize him: "they were so far from recognizing Him, as to take Him for a spirit." They did not expect Christ to come walking on the water; the sight was wholly new, and in their fear and the darkness they took their Saviour for a phantom that had raised the storm. The very One who came to rescue them they mistook for a terror.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the disciples, seeing Christ walking on the sea, took him for a spirit and cried out in terror — the natural reaction of frightened men who did not expect their Lord to come in such a way. Their mistake — fearing as a phantom the very One who came to save them — illustrates how, in the dark of trial, we may mistake Christ's approaches for new terrors, frightening ourselves with creatures of our own imagination. The disciples' cry of fear, like their later fear of the risen Christ (Luke 24:37), would be answered at once by his reassuring word, "It is I; be not afraid" (v.50). The verse marks the depth of their fear before the comfort of his self-revelation, and reminds us that Christ often comes to his people unrecognized, that the cry of distress may be met by the voice of grace.
50:
"For they all saw him, and were troubled. And immediately he talked with them, and saith unto them, Be of good cheer: it is I; be not afraid." The witnesses note the universality of the disciples' fear and the comfort of Christ's self-revelation. Gill: "Had it been only seen by one, it might have been thought a fancy... but as every one saw it, it was out of all doubt that so it was, and which gave them the greater concern." Robertson: "These were the astounding words of cheer. They did not recognize Jesus in the darkness. They had never seen him or any one walk on the water. His voice reassured them."

The word "It is I" the witnesses treasure. Henry (under v.45): "He encouraged them, and silenced their fears, by making himself known to them... It is I; I your Master, I your friend, I your Redeemer and Saviour... The knowledge of Christ, as he is in himself, and near to us, is enough to make the disciples of Christ cheerful even in a storm, and no longer fearful." He notes they knew his voice: "they knew his voice, as the sheep know the voice of their own shepherd (John 10:4)." And he draws out the divine name: "He said, I am he; or I am; it is God's name, when he comes to deliver Israel (Exodus 3:14). So it is Christ's, now that he comes to deliver his disciples." Chrysostom (under v.45): "As soon then as they knew Him by His voice, their fear left them."

The contrast Henry notes: "When Christ said to those that came to apprehend him by force, I am he, they were struck down by it (John 18:6). When he saith to those that come to apprehend him by faith, I am he, they are raised up by it, and comforted."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the disciples' fear was universal — all saw and were troubled — and Christ at once stilled it by making himself known: "Be of good cheer; it is I; be not afraid." The word "It is I" (the very name of God who delivers Israel, Exodus 3:14) both revealed Christ and dispelled their terror, for to know that it is the Master, the Saviour, near at hand, is enough to make his disciples cheerful even in a storm. They knew his voice as sheep know their shepherd's (John 10:4); and the same "I am he" that strikes down his enemies (John 18:6) raises up and comforts those who come to him by faith. The verse is the turning-point of the storm: the cry of terror answered by the voice of grace, fear giving way to courage at the self-revelation of the Lord. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here, on teaching, belongs to Matthew 7.)
51:
"And he went up unto them into the ship; and the wind ceased: and they were sore amazed in themselves beyond measure, and wondered." The witnesses note Christ's entering the ship, the stilling of the wind, and the disciples' excessive amazement. Gill recalls the omitted incident of Peter: "after Peter had desired he might be bid to come to him upon the water, and... beginning to sink, he cried out for help; when Christ stretched out his hand, and saved him; and then he, together with Peter, went up into the ship... though omitted by this evangelist." Robertson notes the omission: "Mark does not give the incident of Peter's walking on the water and beginning to sink. Perhaps Peter was not fond of telling that story." Barnes points to Psalm 93:3-4 (the Lord mightier than the floods).

The stilling of the wind the witnesses note as a quiet but complete miracle. Henry (under v.45): "as soon as he was come into the ship, the wind ceased. In the former storm... He arose, and rebuked the winds (Mark 4:39); but here we read of no such formal command given, only the wind ceased all of a sudden. Note, Our Lord Jesus will be sure to do his own work always effectually, though not always alike solemnly... if thus the wind cease, and we have the comfort of a calm, say, It is because Christ is in the ship."

Theophylact (under v.45) noted the deepening of the miracle: "by entering into the ship, the Lord restrained the tempest... the tempest and the contrary wind were there as well, to make the miracle greater. For the Apostles, not understanding from the miracle of the five loaves the power of Christ, now more fully knew it from the miracle of the sea."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ went up into the ship, the wind ceased at once, and the disciples were utterly amazed. His entering the boat brought the calm — not by a formal rebuke as before, but by his mere presence, teaching that he does his work effectually though not always with the same solemnity. The calm came because Christ was in the ship; and the disciples' deliverance from the storm, joined to his walking on the sea, gave them a fuller knowledge of his power than the feeding had. Mark, drawing on Peter, omits Peter's own faltering walk on the water — perhaps from the apostle's humility. The disciples' excessive amazement (which the next verse will probe) reveals both the greatness of the miracle and the dullness that had not learned from the loaves. The presence of Christ turns the storm to calm; let his people, when the wind ceases, know that it is because their Lord is aboard.
52:
"For they considered not the miracle of the loaves: for their heart was hardened." The witnesses note the cause of the disciples' excessive amazement — their failure to learn from the feeding. Gill: "they did not attend to it, nor learn from it, as they might, the wonderful glory of Christ, and the greatness of his power; which was as much an act of omnipotence, as either his walking upon the water, or causing the wind to cease, or more so." Barnes carefully limits the sense of "hardened": "This does not mean that they were opposed to Jesus... but simply, that they were slow to perceive his power; they did not quickly learn, as they ought to have done, that he had all power." Wesley: "And yet they were not reprobates. It means only, they were slow and dull of apprehension." Robertson: "their failure to grasp the full significance of the miracle of the loaves and fishes, a nature miracle. Here was another, Jesus walking on the water. Their reasoning process was hardened."

Ellicott reads the detail as Peter's own confession: "This is peculiar to St. Mark, and may fairly be received as representing St. Peter's recollection of what had been the mental state of the disciples at the time. They had not drawn from the miracle of the Loaves the conclusion which they might have drawn, that all natural forces were subject to their Master's sovereignty."

Henry (under v.45) drew the lesson: "They were strangely stupid and unthinking, and their heart was hardened, or else they would not have thought it a thing incredible that Christ should command a calm. It is for want of a right understanding of Christ's former works, that we are transported at the thought of his present works, as if there never were the like before." Had they weighed the loaves rightly, the walking on the sea would not have so astonished them, "for his multiplying the bread was as great an instance of his power as his walking on the water."

The witnesses agree: the disciples' excessive amazement sprang from their failure to learn from the feeding — their dull, hardened understanding, which had not drawn from the multiplied loaves the truth that all nature was subject to Christ. The "hardness" was not hostility or reprobation but slowness of apprehension, a failure to reason from one miracle to the next. The lesson, urged by Henry, is for us: it is for want of rightly weighing Christ's former works that we are over-amazed at his present ones, as if there were never the like. Had the disciples understood the loaves, the walking on the sea would have confirmed, not confounded, their faith. The verse, drawn (Ellicott notes) from Peter's candid memory, both confesses the disciples' dullness and warns us to learn from each of Christ's works, that our faith may grow steady and not be perpetually startled.
53:
"And when they had passed over, they came into the land of Gennesaret, and drew to the shore." The witnesses note the landing at the fertile plain of Gennesaret and the people's recognition of Christ. Clarke describes the place: "This country lay on the coast of the sea of Galilee: it is described by Josephus as being exceedingly pleasant and fertile. It had its name of Gennesaret from gen, a garden, and sar, a prince... because of its great fertility." Robertson: "And moored to the shore... They cast anchor or lashed the boat to a post on shore. It was at the plain of Gennesaret several miles south of Bethsaida owing to the night wind."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the healings that follow. Theophylact: "the inhabitants of the country... did not call Him to their houses that He might heal them, but rather the sick themselves were brought to Him... For the miracle which had been wrought on the woman with an issue of blood, had reached the ears of many, and caused in them that great faith, by which they were healed." He reads "the hem of His garment" mystically as "the slightest of His commandments" or "His assumption of our flesh, by which we have come to the Word of God." Bede commends "how great was the faith of the men of the land of Gennesaret, so that they were not content with the healing of those who were present, but sent to other towns round about, that all might hasten to the Physician."

The landing at Gennesaret, after the storm and the walking on the sea, brought Christ to a place of eager faith. The people, recognizing him (v.54), would bring their sick from all the region (v.55) to touch the hem of his garment (v.56) — a faith kindled, the fathers note, by the report of the woman with the issue of blood (Mark 5:27-34), who had been healed by that very touch.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the landing. The witnesses agree: Christ and his disciples, having crossed the sea, landed at the fertile plain of Gennesaret and moored the boat — coming to a place of eager and active faith. The inhabitants, recognizing him and remembering the report of his healings (especially the woman healed by touching his garment), would bring their sick to him from all around. The fathers commend the great faith of the people of Gennesaret, who, not content to be healed themselves, spread the news that all might come to the Physician. The hem of Christ's garment, mystically read as his least commandment or his incarnate flesh, became the point of contact for faith's healing. The landing at Gennesaret opens the closing scene of the chapter — a flood of needy people seeking and finding healing in Christ.
54:
"And when they were come out of the ship, straightway they knew him." The brief verse records the immediate recognition of Christ by the people of Gennesaret. Gill notes it was "Christ and his disciples" who came ashore. Barnes: "They recollected him, for he had been there before, and worked miracles." Clarke: "They recollected him; for he had before preached and wrought miracles in different places of the same country." Robertson: "Recognizing Jesus, knowing fully as nearly all did by now."

The immediate recognition — "straightway they knew him" — the witnesses note as evidence of Christ's fame in that region. He had been there before, preaching and healing; the people knew him by sight and by report. Bede (under v.53): "they knew Him by report, not by His features; or through the greatness of His miracles, even His person was known to some." His reputation as a healer had spread, so that his arrival was at once recognized and acted upon.

The recognition set in motion the flood of healing that follows (vv.55-56). Knowing Christ, and knowing his power and his habit of staying but a little while, the people made haste to bring their sick to him. Henry (under v.45): "they knew what mighty works he did wherever he came, what a universal Healer he was; they knew likewise that he used to stay but a little while at a place, and therefore they were concerned to improve the opportunity."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the moment Christ stepped from the boat, the people of Gennesaret recognized him — by sight and by the fame of his miracles, for he had been among them before. Their immediate recognition, and their knowledge of his power and his brief stays, moved them to seize the opportunity and bring their sick from all the region (vv.55-56). The verse marks the eager response of a people who knew Christ and valued his coming; their prompt action stands in contrast to the dullness of the disciples (v.52) and the rejection of the Gadarenes (Mark 5:17). To know Christ and his power should move us, as it moved them, to bring to him at once all our need. The recognition of Christ is the beginning of the healing; those who know him hasten to him while the opportunity lasts.
55:
"And ran through that whole region round about, and began to carry about in beds those that were sick, where they heard he was." The witnesses note the eager, energetic pursuit of healing by the people of Gennesaret. Gill: "the inhabitants of that part of the country where Christ landed, ran many of them themselves, and others sent messengers into all parts of it, on every side." Robertson: "Vivid constative aorist picturing the excited pursuit of Jesus as the news spread that he was in Gennesaret. On their beds — pallets like that of the man let down through the roof (Mark 2:4). Where they heard he was — imperfect tense (repetition)."

The carrying of the sick on beds the witnesses note as a mark of earnest faith and love. Henry (under v.45): "they were concerned to improve the opportunity of this kind visit which he made them; They ran through that whole region round about, with all possible expedition, and began to carry about in beds those that were sick, and not able to go themselves; there was no danger of their getting cold when they hoped to get a cure." Bede (under v.53) commended "how great was the faith of the men of the land of Gennesaret, so that they were not content with the healing of those who were present, but sent to other towns round about, that all might hasten to the Physician."

The energy of the pursuit — running through the whole region, carrying the bedridden, tracking Christ wherever he went — displays a faith that would not let the opportunity pass. The people knew Christ's power and his brief stays, and they spared no effort to bring every sufferer to him.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the people of Gennesaret, recognizing Christ, ran through the whole region and carried their sick on beds to wherever they heard he was — an eager, energetic pursuit born of faith in his healing power. Their love for the suffering and their faith in Christ would not be content to let any opportunity slip; they bore the bedridden from place to place, tracking the Healer's movements. The scene of the whole countryside running with their sick to Christ is a picture of active faith, which spares no labour to bring the needy to the One who can heal. It stands as a rebuke to spiritual indolence and a pattern of zeal: those who know Christ's power will move heaven and earth to bring themselves and others to him. The verse prepares for the summary of universal healing (v.56), in which all who touched even the hem of his garment were made whole.
56:
"And whithersoever he entered, into villages, or cities, or country, they laid the sick in the streets, and besought him that they might touch if it were but the border of his garment: and as many as touched him were made whole." The witnesses note the universal scope of Christ's healing and the faith that touched his garment. Ellicott parses the places: "farms or hamlets. The three words form almost an exhaustive list of the various grades of aggregate human habitations. In the streets — better, in the market-places. The border of his garment — better, the hem, or fringe." On "were made whole," he notes "the Greek tense implies an event frequently recurring." Barnes and Clarke cross-reference the fringe (Numbers 15:38-39) and the woman with the issue of blood (Mark 5:27-28).

The touching of the hem the witnesses connect to the earlier miracle. Henry (under v.45): "they laid the sick in the streets, to be in his way, and begged leave for them to touch if it were but the border of his garment, as the woman with the bloody issue did, by whom, it should seem, this method of application was first brought in; and as many as touched, were made whole." Theophylact (under v.53) had read the hem mystically as "the slightest of His commandments" or "His assumption of our flesh, by which we have come to the Word of God."

Clarke draws the pattern for ministry: "Christ went about doing good — he confined his ministry and miracles to no place — wherever he went, they stood in need of his help; and whenever they required his assistance, they had it granted immediately. Our Lord's conduct, in these respects, is a perfect pattern for every preacher of his Gospel." Henry notes the limit of the people's desire: "We do not find that they were desirous to be taught by him, only to be healed... it is sad to think how much more concerned the most of men are about their bodies than about their souls."

The witnesses agree: wherever Christ went — villages, cities, or farms — the people laid their sick in the marketplaces and begged to touch even the hem of his garment, and as many as touched were healed. The universal scope of his healing, and the faith that, learning from the woman with the issue of blood, sought only to touch his fringe, display both the boundless compassion and power of Christ and the eager faith of the people. Yet the witnesses note, with Henry, the sober qualification: they sought healing for their bodies, not teaching for their souls — a reminder that men are commonly more concerned for the body than the soul. Christ's tireless going about, healing all who touched him, is the perfect pattern of gospel ministry; and the hem of his garment, the point of contact for faith, is read by the fathers as his incarnate flesh, by which we come to the Word of God. The chapter closes with Christ the universal Healer, freely answering every touch of faith.
